# V. The Class Struggle

## 1. Socialism and the Propertied Classes

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The final paragraphs of the general part of our present programme read:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">„This social transformation means the emancipation not merely of the proletariat, but of the entire human race, which suffers under present conditions. But it can only be the work of the working class, because all other classes, despite the conflicts of interest among themselves, stand upon the ground of private property in the means of production and have the preservation of the foundations of present-day society as their common goal.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">The struggle of the working class against capitalist exploitation is necessarily a political struggle. The working class cannot conduct its economic struggles and cannot develop its economic organisation without political rights. It cannot bring about the transfer of the means of production into the possession of the community without having come into possession of political power.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">To shape this struggle of the working class into a conscious and unified one and to point out to it its naturally necessary goal – that is the task of the Social Democratic Party.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">The interests of the working class are the same in all countries with a capitalist mode of production. With the expansion of world commerce and of production for the world market, the condition of the workers of every country becomes ever more dependent on the condition of the workers in the other countries. The emancipation of the working class is thus a work in which the workers of all civilised countries are equally involved. In recognition of this, the Social Democratic Party of Germany feels and declares itself one with the class-conscious workers of all other countries.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">The Social Democratic Party of Germany therefore fights not for new class privileges and prerogatives, but for the abolition of class rule and of the classes themselves, and for equal rights and equal duties of all without distinction of sex and descent. Proceeding from these views, it combats in present-day society not merely the exploitation and oppression of wage labourers, but every kind of exploitation and oppression, whether it be directed against a class, a party, a sex or a race.“</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The introductory sentence of the first of these paragraphs scarcely requires any further elucidation. We have already demonstrated in detail that the displacement of capitalist production by socialist production lies not merely in the interest of the propertyless and the exploited, but also in the interest of the entire social development, and thereby in a certain sense even in the interest of the propertied and the exploiters. These too suffer under the contradictions to which the modern mode of production gives rise. Some of them degenerate in idleness, others wear themselves out in a breathless chase after profit, and over all of them there always hovers the Damoclean sword of bankruptcy, of sinking into the proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But appearances teach us that the great mass of the propertied and the exploiting confront socialism not merely with doubt and mistrust, but even in the most embittered hostility.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Could mere lack of knowledge and insight be to blame for this? But the spokesmen among the opponents of socialism are precisely those whose position in the state, in society, in science ought to make them most capable of grasping the social connections and understanding the direction of social development.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And so glaring are the conditions of present-day society that no one who wishes to be taken seriously in politics or in science any longer dares to deny the justification of the socialist critique. On the contrary, the most enlightened minds in all non-socialist parties acknowledge that it has a „justified kernel“, indeed some of them even declare the victory of socialism to be inevitable – but only conditionally, namely, inevitable only if society does not suddenly reverse course and improve itself, which can happen entirely at will and with ease if one hastens to do justice to the particular wishes of one or the other of these parties.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In this way even those members of the non-socialist parties who have best grasped the socialist critique evade, by a logical sidestep at precisely the decisive point, the necessity of drawing the consequences of this critique.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The reason for this curious phenomenon is not difficult to recognise. Even though certain interests, not to be underestimated, of the propertied themselves speak against private property in the means of production, other, much more immediate and more easily comprehensible interests of theirs demand the maintenance of this private property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This applies above all, of course, to the <em>rich.</em> They can gain nothing directly from the abolition of private property in the means of production. Beneficial social consequences must indeed result from it, which would benefit them too, but these lie comparatively far off. In contrast, the disadvantages that the abolition of this private property must bring them are evident from the outset; in power and prestige they must decidedly lose, some of them perhaps also in comfort and easy living, according to the circumstances under which the social revolution takes place.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is otherwise with the lower strata of the propertied, with the exploited among them, the small artisans, small peasants, etc. These have nothing to forfeit in power and prestige, and in easy living they can only gain through the introduction and development of the socialist mode of production. But in order to be able to grasp this, they must raise themselves above the horizon of the classes to which they belong. From the standpoint of the narrow-minded petty bourgeois or small peasant, the capitalist mode of production is incomprehensible, though they feel its effects on their own bodies, and even more incomprehensible for them is modern socialism. What they do readily grasp, on the other hand, is the necessity of private property in the means of production for their own mode of operation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As long as the artisan feels himself an artisan, the peasant a peasant, the small trader a small trader, as long as they possess a vigorous class consciousness, they must cling to private property in the means of production and remain inaccessible to socialism, however badly things may go for them.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have seen in an earlier chapter how private property in the means of production chains the declining petty bourgeois and small peasants to their backward enterprises, even when these have long ceased to be able to provide them with any tolerably adequate existence, and even when they would improve their position by passing over to wage labour. Thus private property is also the power which chains all propertied classes to the present mode of production, even those who at the same time belong to the exploited, even those whose property is now only a mocking caricature of the concept of &#8222;property&#8220;.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only those among the petty bourgeois and small peasants who despair of the continued existence of their class, who no longer close their minds to the conviction that the forms of enterprise upon which their existence rests are doomed to destruction—only they are capable of grasping the doctrines of socialism. But the lack of education and the narrowness of their horizon, the natural consequences of their conditions of life, make it very difficult for them to attain the necessary insight into the hopelessness of their class situation. Their misery, their convulsive search for a means that might deliver them from it, has hitherto for the most part accomplished nothing other than to make them easy prey for every demagogue who displayed the requisite self-assurance and did not fail to offer glittering promises.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the higher strata of the propertied classes more education and a broader outlook are to be found. Also, in many an educated person there still lingers a remnant of the old idealism from the time of the revolutionary struggles of the rising bourgeoisie, the age of the Enlightenment. But woe to the bourgeois who allows himself to be led into taking an interest in socialism and acting upon it! He soon faces the choice of either abandoning his ideas or tearing apart all the social bonds which until then have not merely fettered him but also sustained him. Only a few preserve enough courage and independence to press forward to this parting of the ways, and only the very fewest of these few possess the strength, once they have arrived there, to break decisively with their class. But of these few of the few, the greater part have hitherto tended to grow weary all too soon; later they came to recognise their &#8222;youthful follies&#8220; and became &#8222;sensible&#8220;.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The bourgeois <em>idealists</em> are the only ones among the members of the higher bourgeoisie of whom it is at all possible that they should become adherents of socialism. But for the great majority of those of these idealists who have acquired a deeper insight into social conditions and into the problems arising from them, the insight gained forms merely the occasion to exhaust themselves in a fruitless search for a so-called &#8222;peaceful&#8220; solution of the &#8222;social question&#8220;, a solution which reconciles the demands of their more or less socialist knowledge and conscience with the class interests of the bourgeoisie—which is just as impossible as a wet fire or a burning water.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only those bourgeois idealists who have not merely attained the necessary theoretical insight but have also already broken with the bourgeoisie at least inwardly, and who possess the courage and the strength to break with it outwardly as well, are able to develop into true socialists.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>From the propertied classes, then, the cause of socialism has not much to expect. Individual members of them may be won for socialism, but only such as no longer belong, according to their consciousness, to the class to which their economic position assigns them. That will always be a small minority, except in revolutionary times, when the scales seem to incline to the side of socialism. Then, indeed, a serious desertion is likely to break out in the ranks of the propertied classes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But hitherto the only fruitful recruiting grounds of the socialist armies have not been the classes of those who still had something, even if perhaps not much, to lose, but of those who &#8222;have nothing to lose but their chains, who have a world to win&#8220;.<br/> <a id="ch5-t2" name="ch5-t2"> </a></p>
```

## 2. Domestic Servants and the Servant Class

```{=html}
<p class="fst">By no means, however, are all strata of the propertyless to be regarded as productive recruiting grounds for Social Democracy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We cannot, of course, give here a natural history of the proletariat; we have already said the most important things about it in Chapter II, where we considered the role of the proletariat in the present-day mode of production. Here let us give only a few supplementary remarks that seem to us necessary in order to explain the role played by the various strata of the proletariat in the economic and political struggles of our time.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We already know that, even though the saying beloved of the philistine—that there have always been poor people—is false, it must nevertheless be acknowledged that poverty is as old as commodity production. To be sure, it formerly constituted, as a rule, only an isolated phenomenon. In the Middle Ages, for example, the number of those who did not possess the means of production necessary for their own economic operation was small. Of the few propertyless, the majority easily succeeded in finding acceptance in a propertied family as helpers, farmhands, journeymen, maids; for the most part these were younger people to whom the prospect still beckoned of one day being able to establish their own economic operation and their own hearth. In any case, they worked together with the head of the family or his wife, and enjoyed together with them the fruits of their labour. As members of a propertied family they were not proletarians; they felt themselves in solidarity with the property of the family, in whose advantages they shared, by whose injuries they too were harmed. And this still holds true today in those remote regions where such a patriarchal relationship has been preserved. Where the domestic servants still belong to the family of the propertied person, they defend property, although they themselves are propertyless, and there socialism finds no ground.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Matters stood similarly with the artisan journeymen. (Cf. Chap. II, 1.)</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Alongside the class of farmhands and journeymen there arose the <em>servant class.</em> A portion of the propertyless turned to the families of the greater <em>exploiters,</em> in the Middle Ages and the beginnings of the modern era especially to the nobles and princes, the higher clergy and the merchants. They entered their pay, not to help them at work, but to protect them and to serve their opulence, as men-at-arms and lackeys. The common sharing of work and enjoyment, the patriarchal relationship, was lacking here, and with it also the solidarity between master and servant that springs from it. But a solidarity of a different kind developed between the master and the servant. Where a larger body of servants is maintained, there are also <em>rank differences</em> within it. The individual has to hope for promotion, for an increase in income, power and prestige. This increase, however, depends on the whim of his master. The more skilfully he adapts and subordinates himself to it, and the more he outdoes his fellow servants, the better his prospects. Thus the servant feels himself in solidarity with the master, but as a secret enemy of all his fellow workers. And yet another solidarity forms between the master and the servant. The greater the master's income, power and prestige, the more of all this falls to the servant. This holds especially of the actual luxury servants, who have nothing to do but to "represent," to prove how much superfluous money their master has at his disposal, and to help him to squander it as quickly and pleasantly as possible, by serving him fearlessly and "faithfully" in all his follies and vices. The servant therefore feels himself in solidarity with the exploiter and oppressor against the exploited and oppressed; indeed, he easily behaves toward them even more ruthlessly than the master himself. For the master, if he is at all reasonable, will not slaughter the hen that lays him golden eggs; he wants to preserve it not only for himself but also for his descendants. For the servant this consideration does not exist.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>No wonder that nothing is more hated among the people than the servant class, that its fawning upward and brutality downward are proverbial. By a servant's soul one understands the epitome of all wretchedness.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Naturally these influences upon the character of the servant class are operative not only in the propertyless person from the lower estates, but also in the propertyless or little-propertied person from the higher estates, such as the impoverished nobleman who seeks his fortune as a higher servant, a courtier at a princely court. <a href="#ch5-n9" id="ch5-f9" name="ch5-f9">[9]</a></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Here, however, we are dealing only with the lower servants, and must therefore refrain, however tempting it might be, from pursuing the comparison with the noble servants—a comparison which, incidentally, suggests itself and which is not hard to spin out further. What matters here is to demonstrate why the servant class, although it belongs to the propertyless, forms no particularly promising recruiting ground for socialism. It is, rather, a bulwark of existing society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The growth of exploitation, of the mass of surplus value annually produced, and the resulting increase of luxury, favours a steady multiplication of the servant class. But fortunately for social development, its warlike variety, the <em>mercenary soldiery,</em> has greatly declined since the transformation of the military constitution which the French Revolution initiated, since the replacement of the mercenary army by the army of universal conscription. To be sure, it has by no means wholly disappeared, and it is due not least to its remnants in the modern armies that the "nation in arms" has hitherto in most cases proved to be a by no means democratic institution.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the growth of the actual servant class and of domestic servants in general is likewise counteracted, despite the steady increase of luxury, by a strong tendency: the dissolution of the traditional family form and the division of labour, which assigns ever more tasks of the household and of personal service to separate, independent occupations: hairdressers, waiters, hired cabmen, porters, etc. These occupations branched off from the servant class do indeed still display, long after they have become independent, the character marks of their origin, but gradually they nevertheless begin to take on the qualities and views of the industrial wage-labouring class.<br/> <a id="ch5-t3" name="ch5-t3"> </a></p>
```

## 3. The Lumpenproletariat

```{=html}
<p class="fst">However numerous the domestic and luxury servants as well as the journeymen and mercenaries may have been at times, they were as a rule not in a position to absorb all those without property. Those without property who were <em>incapable of work</em> – children, the aged, the sick, cripples – were from the outset in no position to find their livelihood in one of these occupations. But there gathered around them, as we have seen, from the beginnings of modern times such a mass of people seeking work – especially peasants driven from their farms or fleeing from ill-treatment – that numerous able-bodied persons fell into the same situation as those incapable of work. Nothing remained for them but to beg, to steal, or to prostitute themselves. They were confronted with the choice of starving or acting contrary to all the prevailing conceptions of shame, honour, and dignity. They could only eke out their lives by continually placing concern for their most immediate personal needs above concern for their good reputation. That such a condition must have a highly demoralising and corrupting effect is clear.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This corruption was, and still is, further increased by the fact that the workless poor are utterly superfluous to society, that society not only does not need them but, on the contrary, would be rid of an unwelcome burden through their removal. But every class must degenerate that is superfluous, that has no necessary functions to perform in society; this applies to the lowest as well as to the highest.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And the beggars cannot even raise themselves through the self-deception that they are necessary; they possess no memories of a time when their class rendered society a service; they cannot, insisting on their power, force their parasitism upon society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>They are merely tolerated: humility is therefore the first duty of the beggar, it is regarded as the highest virtue of the poor. Like the servants, this kind of proletarian is also servile towards the powerful; they form no opposition to the existing social order. On the contrary. They are dependent on the crumbs that fall from the rich man's table: how could they wish that the rich man should vanish! They themselves are not exploited; but the greater the degree of the exploitation of the worker, the greater the income of the rich man, the more generous he can be, the more the poor have to expect from him. They partake in the exploitation, like the servants; what cause would they have to combat it? In the beginnings of the Reformation in Germany, when the Catholic Church was hated in the extreme by all classes because it exploited them all, it was these proletarians who clung faithfully to it, for they received from it more plentiful alms than from the stingy townsfolk and the plundered peasants.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This kind of proletariat – the <em>lumpenproletariat</em> – has never yet confronted exploitation of its own accord. But of course, it is not a bulwark of exploitation either, like the warlike variety of the servant class. Cowardly and without principle, it deserts without hesitation those whose alms it has pocketed, as soon as they lose their wealth and power. It has never stood in the vanguard of a revolutionary movement, but it was at once at hand in all disturbances to fish in troubled waters. It has sometimes helped to give the final blow to a ruling class that was in the act of falling. As a rule, in a revolution it has contented itself with exploiting and compromising it, in order to betray it at the first opportunity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist mode of production has greatly increased the lumpenproletariat; it constantly supplies it with new recruits; it forms, especially in the large cities, a considerable part of the population.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Very close to it in character and outlook is that part of the small peasantry and petty bourgeoisie which has come down utterly in the world, which despairs of its own strength and seeks to keep its head above water by means of alms thrown to it by the upper classes.<br/> <a id="ch5-t4" name="ch5-t4"> </a></p>
```

## 4. The Beginnings of the Wage Proletariat

```{=html}
<p class="fst">It was these latter strata from which nascent capitalist production, and above all <em>large-scale industry,</em> preferred to draw its labour-power. This industry demands less <em>skilled</em> workers than <em>patient, unresisting</em> workers who fit themselves without will into the great machinery of a modern factory, which can only function without disturbance if each of its countless little cogs punctually and steadily carries out the movements assigned to it. And just as the strata of the working classes nearest to the lumpenproletariat—indeed some parts of the lumpenproletariat itself—supplied the majority of the workers of the emerging capitalist large-scale industry, so too the treatment which these strata put up with became decisive for the treatment which the capitalists wished to mete out to their workers in general. Labour itself, whose ennobling influence bourgeois economists and moralists so gladly describe, became for the proletarians at first a source of further degradation, not of elevation. The workers' lack of resistance made it possible for the capitalists to extend working time to the utmost. What powerful motives in capitalist large-scale industry drove them to this we have already seen in Chapter II. Capital does not grant the proletarian time to live and to educate himself, if it is not obliged to. Where it finds no barriers, it lets labour continue to the point of complete exhaustion. If a short pause remains between work and sleep, then it suffices only for the most fleeting pleasures, so as thereby to numb the consciousness of misery—for the intoxication of alcohol or of sexual intercourse. The working together of men and women, of adults and children, which among joyful, free, and duty-conscious human beings can be a source of the highest intellectual stimulation and moral ennoblement for all involved, became in the capitalist factory at first only a means of increasing the danger of contagion through its demoralising and enervating influences, of letting depravity spread still more rapidly in the proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>No wonder that the working proletarians in the beginnings of capitalist large-scale industry scarcely differed from the lumpenproletarians. How deeply they were sunk in crime, drunkenness, brutality, and filth—physical and mental—is best seen from the classic description that <em>Friedrich Engels</em> gave of the condition of the working classes of England in the first decades of this century. <a href="#ch5-n10" id="ch5-f10" name="ch5-f10">[10]</a><br/> <a id="ch5-t5" name="ch5-t5"> </a></p>
```

## 5. The Rise of the Wage Proletariat

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The concept of the proletarian seemed to be synonymous with that of the most extreme degradation. There are still people today who hold this view, among them some who fancy themselves to be very modern. And yet, already at the time when the working proletariat outwardly had so many features in common with the lumpenproletariat, a deep gulf was forming between the two.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The lumpenproletariat has essentially always remained the same, whenever and wherever it appeared as a mass phenomenon. The lumpenproletariat of present-day Berlin or London does not differ all that greatly from that of ancient Rome. The modern working proletariat, by contrast, is a quite peculiar phenomenon, such as world history has not yet seen.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Between the lumpenproletariat and the working proletariat of capitalist production there exists above all the enormous, fundamental difference that the former is a parasite, whereas the latter is one of the roots of society—and indeed a root that increasingly becomes not only the most important but ultimately also the <em>only</em> one from which society draws its strength. The working proletarian is propertyless, but no <em>recipient of alms.</em> Far from being maintained by society, he maintains it through his labour. In the beginnings of capitalist production, to be sure, the working proletarian still feels himself to be a poor man; in the capitalist who exploits him he sees his benefactor, who gives him work and thereby also bread, the bread-giver, the employer. This "patriarchal" relationship is naturally very agreeable to the capitalists. To this day they demand from their workers, in return for the wage they pay them, not merely the stipulated work performance, but also submissiveness and gratitude.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But capitalist production can nowhere last long without the fine patriarchal conditions of its beginnings going to the devil. However enslaved and dulled the workers may be, sooner or later they nonetheless notice that they are the bread-givers of the capitalist and not the other way around. While they remain poor or perhaps grow even poorer, the capitalist becomes ever richer. And when they ask the manufacturer, this alleged patriarch, for more bread, he gives them a stone.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The working proletarians differ from the lumpenproletarians and the servants in that they do not live off the exploitation practised by the exploiters; from the household servants and the journeymen artisans (cf. Chapter II) they differ in that they neither work together nor live together with their exploiter, that every personal relationship between the exploited and the exploiter has disappeared for them. They live in wretched holes and build their exploiter a palace; they go hungry and prepare him a sumptuous meal. They toil until they collapse from exhaustion, in order to provide him and his family with the means to kill time.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is a quite different antagonism from that between the rich man and the "little man," the poor man of the pre-capitalist era. The latter envies the rich man, to whom he looks up with admiration, who is his model, his ideal. He would like to be in his place, an exploiter like him. It does not occur to him to want to abolish exploitation. The working proletarian does not envy the rich man, he does not wish himself in his place; <em>he hates him and despises him;</em> he hates him as his exploiter and despises him as a drone. At first he hates merely the particular capitalist with whom he happens to be dealing, but he soon recognizes that they all, by and large, proceed against him in the same manner, and his initial personal hatred develops into a conscious antagonism towards the entire capitalist class.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This antagonism towards the exploiting class is one of the earliest characteristics of the working proletariat. Class hatred is by no means a result of socialist propaganda—it made itself felt long before the latter's activity in the working class. Among servants, household servants, and journeymen artisans, such an intense class hatred is impossible. Given the intimate personal relationships of the members of these occupations to their "masters," such hatred would make any fruitful activity impossible for them. In these occupations there are enough conflicts between the wage labourers and the heads of enterprise and household; but reconciliation always follows. In the capitalist mode of production the workers can harbour the most embittered hostility towards the entrepreneur without production thereby being disrupted, indeed even without his noticing anything of it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This hatred at first expresses itself only timidly and only in isolated cases. If it takes some time before the proletarians notice that it is anything but nobility of mind that moves the manufacturers to employ them, it takes even longer before they find the <em>courage</em> to confront the "master" openly in a conflict.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The lumpenproletarian is cowardly and humble because he feels himself superfluous and is deprived of any material support. The same character traits are initially exhibited also by the working proletariat, insofar as it recruits itself from the lumpenproletariat and the strata close to it. It does indeed feel all the mistreatment meted out to it, but it protests against it only in secret; it clenches its fist in its pocket. Alongside this, the indignation of especially energetic and passionate natures vents itself in secret crimes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The consciousness of their own strength and the spirit of resistance develop in the strata of the wage-labouring class here under discussion only when they arrive at the consciousness of the community of interest, of the <em>solidarity</em> that prevails among their members. With the awakening of the <em>feeling of solidarity</em> begins the moral rebirth of the proletariat, the rise of the working proletariat out of the swamp of the lumpenproletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The working conditions of capitalist production of themselves point the proletarians towards the necessity of holding firmly together, of the subordination of the individual to the whole. Whereas in the artisan trade in its classic form each individual creates a whole for himself alone, capitalist industry rests on working together, on <em>cooperation.</em> There the individual worker can accomplish nothing without his comrades. If they take up the work in a united and systematic manner, then the productive capacity of each individual among them is doubled and tripled. Thus labour brings home to them the power of association; thus labour develops in them a voluntary, joyful discipline, which is the precondition of a cooperative, a socialist production, but which is also a precondition of every successful struggle of the proletariat against exploitation in capitalist production. That production itself thereby trains the proletariat for its own overthrow and for work in the socialist society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Perhaps even more powerful than cooperation in awakening the feeling of solidarity in the proletariat is the <em>equality of the working conditions</em>. In a factory there are, as a rule, virtually no gradations of rank among the workers, no hierarchy. The higher posts are generally inaccessible to the proletarians, but always so few in number that they are of no account for the mass of the workers. Only a very few can be corrupted by these positions of favour. For the great majority the same working conditions prevail, and the individual has no possibility of improving them for himself alone; he can only raise his own position if that of the whole, that of all his fellow workers, is raised. The manufacturers do indeed attempt to sow discord among the workers by artificially bringing about inequalities in the working conditions. But the levelling effect of modern large-scale industry is too strong for such expedients — piece-work, bonuses and the like — to be able in the long run to deaden the consciousness of the solidarity of interests among the workers. The longer capitalist production lasts, the more vigorously proletarian solidarity develops, the more deeply it takes root in the proletariat, and the more it becomes its most conspicuous characteristic.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We need only point to what we said above about the servant class in order to show how greatly the working proletariat differs from it in this respect. But the domestic servants also fall short of the proletariat of capitalist production in this regard, indeed even the journeymen of the crafts do so.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The solidarity of the journeymen halted at a point that the solidarity of the proletarians has crossed. The solidarity of both alike is not confined to the workers of the same enterprise; like the proletarians, the journeymen too gradually came to the recognition that the workers everywhere encounter the same adversaries, everywhere have the same interests. The journeymen established national organisations, embracing the whole territory of the nation, at a time when the bourgeoisie was still deeply mired in petty provincialism and particularism. The proletariat of today is wholly international in its feeling and its action; in the midst of the most embittered nationality struggles, the most zealous war preparations of the ruling classes, the proletarians of all countries have united.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We already find the beginnings of international organisations among the journeymen; they showed themselves capable of overcoming national narrowness. But over <em>one</em> barrier they were unable to raise themselves: the <em>trade.</em> The German hatter or coppersmith might, on his wanderings, find a hospitable reception among his colleagues in Sweden or Switzerland; the shoemakers or joiners of his own country, of his own native town, on the other hand, remained strangers to him. The trades were, precisely, strictly separated in the crafts. For years the apprentice had to learn until he attained journeyman status, and then throughout his life he remained faithful to his craft. Its flourishing and its power were also his own. If the journeyman stood in a certain opposition to the master of his craft, he stood in no less opposition to the masters and journeymen of other crafts. In the heyday of the crafts we find the journeymen's associations of the various crafts embroiled in fierce struggles and hostilities among themselves.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capitalist production, by contrast, jumbles the various trades colourfully together. In a capitalist enterprise workers of different trades usually work side by side and together for the attainment of a common purpose. On the other hand, it has the tendency to abolish the concept of the trade in production altogether. The machine shortens the former years-long apprenticeship of the worker to a training period of a few weeks, often days. It enables the individual worker to pass from one manipulation to another without too great difficulty. It often compels him to do so, by making him superfluous in his previous occupation, throwing him onto the pavement and forcing him to look about for another. The freedom of choice of occupation, which the philistine fears he will lose in the "state of the future", has for the worker already today lost all meaning.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under these circumstances it is easy for him to cross the barrier before which the journeymen halted. If the consciousness of solidarity of the modern proletariat is an international one, it also extends to the <em>whole working class.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Various forms of wage labour existed already in antiquity and the Middle Ages. The struggles between wage labourers and their exploiters are likewise nothing new. But only under the domination of capitalist large-scale industry do we see a unified class of wage labourers arise who are well aware of the community of their interests and who subordinate not only their personal but also their local and — so far as they still exist — their occupational special interests more and more to the great collective interests of the <em>class.</em> Only in our century do the struggles of the wage labourers against exploitation acquire the character of a <em>class struggle.</em> And only through this is it possible that these struggles receive a wider, higher goal than that of the redress of momentary grievances, that the workers' movement becomes a <em>revolutionary</em> movement.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the concept of the working class becomes ever wider. In the first place, what has been said here applies to the working proletarians of large-scale industry. But just as industrial capital becomes ever more decisive for the whole of capital, indeed for all economic undertakings within the domain of the capitalist nations, so the thinking and feeling of the proletariat working in large-scale industry becomes ever more decisive for the thinking and feeling of the wage labourers in general. The consciousness of the general community of interests seizes also the workers of capitalist manufacture and of the crafts, and the latter all the sooner, the more the craft loses its original character and approaches manufacture or sinks down to the level of the capitalistically exploited domestic industry.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To them are joined, little by little, the workers of the non-industrial urban trades, those of commerce, of transport, of "lodging and refreshment", as the German occupational statistics put it. The rural workers too gradually become conscious of their community of interests with the other wage labourers, as soon as capitalist production dissolves the old patriarchal management of agriculture and makes of it an industry that produces with wage proletarians, no longer with servants belonging to the farmer's family. Indeed, finally the feeling of solidarity begins to seize the worse-placed among the independent craftsmen, and under certain circumstances even to infect peasants: thus the working classes are welded together more and more into a single unified <em>working class</em>, which is animated by the spirit of the proletariat of large-scale industry, which steadily increases in number and economic importance. There spreads within it more and more that spirit, peculiar to the large-industrial proletariat, of comradely cohesion, of cooperative discipline, of opposition to capital; but there also spreads within its ranks that insatiable thirst for knowledge peculiar to the proletariat, to which we pointed at the close of the previous chapter.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus there gradually arises out of the despised, maltreated, degraded proletariat a new world-historical power, before which the old powers begin to tremble: a new class emerges with a new morality and a new philosophy, increasing daily in number, in cohesion, in economic indispensability, in self-consciousness and in insight.<br/> <a id="ch5-t6" name="ch5-t6"> </a></p>
```

## 6. The Conflict of the Tendencies Elevating the Proletariat and Those Depressing It

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The elevation of the proletariat out of its degradation is an inevitable, naturally necessary process. But it is neither a peaceful nor an even one. The tendencies of the capitalist mode of production, as we have seen in Chapter II, tend to depress the working population ever further. The moral regeneration of the proletariat is only possible in <em>conflict</em> against these tendencies and their bearers, the capitalists. It is only possible in so far as the counter-effects, the counter-tendencies, grow sufficiently strong, which are generated in the bosom of the proletariat by the new conditions under which it works and lives. But the depressing tendencies of the capitalist mode of production are very different at different times, in different regions, in different branches of industry; they depend on the state of the market, on the degree of competition of the individual enterprises with one another, on the level of development of machinery in the branches of industry concerned, on the measure of insight the capitalists have into their lasting interests, and so on, and so on. The counter-effects that develop in the bosom of the individual proletarian strata likewise depend on the most manifold conditions: on the habits and needs of the classes of the population from which the proletarians in question are chiefly recruited; on the degree of skill or strength which the labour requires in the branch of industry in which they are active; on the extent of the labour of women and children; on the size of the industrial reserve army, which is by no means the same for all occupations; on the insight of the workers; and finally on whether the labour brings with it a dispersal and separation, or a uniting and gathering together of the workers, and so on.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Each of these conditions is highly different for the various branches of industry and strata of workers, and is subject to constant change, since the technical and economic revolution proceeds without interruption. Daily new regions and new branches of occupation are subjected by capital to exploitation and proletarianisation, daily new branches of production are created, and the existing ones are ceaselessly revolutionised. As in the beginnings of the capitalist mode of production, so too today we see again and again new strata of the population sinking into the proletariat, going under in the lumpenproletariat, and again and again new strata rising out of it; within the working proletariat itself a constant surge upward and downward is perceptible, some strata moving in an ascending, others in a descending direction, according as the depressing or the elevating tendencies prevail among them.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But fortunately for the further development of human society, sooner or later the moment arrives, in most proletarian strata, when the elevating tendencies decisively gain the upper hand; and once these tendencies have been active in a proletarian stratum to such a degree that they have awakened in it self-consciousness, class consciousness, the consciousness of the solidarity of all its members with one another and with the entire working class, the consciousness of the strength that springs from firm cohesion; once they have reared up in this proletarian stratum the consciousness of its economic indispensability and its self-respect; once they have awakened in it the conviction that the working class is moving towards a better future: as soon as a proletarian stratum has thus elevated itself, it becomes infinitely difficult to thrust it down again into the dull mass of those degraded existences that may indeed hate, but cannot band together for enduring struggle, that, despairing of themselves and their future, seek forgetfulness in intoxication, that draw from their sufferings not the urge to defiant revolt but to timid submission. It is almost impossible to eradicate class consciousness in a proletarian stratum once it has firmly taken root in it. However heavily the depressing tendencies of the capitalist mode of production may then make themselves felt, they can bring this stratum down <em>economically</em>, but not <em>morally</em> – unless the pressure be no longer merely depressing but wholly crushing, as in some decaying domestic industries. In every other case the pressure will only have the effect of producing counter-pressure; it now acts less to stunt than to embitter; the proletarian is thereby no longer depressed into a rogue, but elevated into a martyr.<br/> <a id="ch5-t7" name="ch5-t7"> </a></p>
```

## 7. Philanthropy and Labour Protection Legislation

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Were every stratum of the proletariat dependent solely on its own forces, then for the majority of them the process of ascent would begin far later and take an even more protracted and painful form than is actually the case. Without outside help many a stratum of the proletariat that now occupies a respectable position would never have managed to overcome the difficulties which, like all beginnings, also inhere in the beginnings of the ascent from the mire into which the proletariat is hurled by capitalist development. The help came to these proletarian strata from certain strata of society standing above them, from the higher strata of the working <em>proletariat</em> as well as from the <em>propertied classes.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The latter help was, particularly in the beginnings of large-scale capitalist industry, not without significance.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the Middle Ages poverty was so slight that public (particularly ecclesiastical) and private charity sufficed to cope with it. It posed no riddles to be solved; insofar as it gave occasion for reflection, it prompted at most edifying contemplations. It was regarded as a pedagogical instrument of the dear Lord; it counted, when those afflicted by it were sinful, as a rod of chastisement, and when they were pious, as a trial, in order to make their trust in God triumph all the more gloriously. For the rich, however, poverty was a drill-ground for their virtues, one that appeared just as necessary for the salvation of their soul as, say, a tournament-ground for the steeling of their body.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But when, through the development of commodity production, the old feudal agriculture was dissolved, when the migration of the released peasants to the towns began and there "overpopulation," unemployment, mass poverty began to spread, then this phenomenon, as new as it was terrible and fraught with danger, drew upon itself the attention of all thinking and feeling people. Against mass poverty the medieval means of charity did not suffice: the Reformation moreover stopped up the most important source of alms, the poor-relief of the Catholic Church. To provide for all the poor appeared more and more as a task surpassing the powers of society; a new social problem arose: <em>the abolition of poverty.</em> The most varied solutions to it were devised, according to the insight and the humanity of the devisers, from the convenient method of doing away with poverty by clearing the poor out of the way (say, by the gallows or by deportation), up to the deeply considered plans for a new, a communist society. These latter found great applause among the educated, but the convenient methods were the only ones to which the various fathers of the land and sages of state could bring themselves. Yet all the more heads grew back to poverty, the more proletarians one beheaded and branded.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But gradually the question of poverty took on yet another face. The capitalist mode of production had arisen and began to extend itself ever further, to become ever more the dominant one in society. With that, the problem of the abolition of poverty ceased to exist for the thinkers of the bourgeoisie. Capitalist production rests upon the proletariat; to abolish the latter means to make capitalist production impossible. Mass poverty is the foundation of capitalist mass wealth; whoever wishes to check mass poverty lays hands on wealth. Whoever today wishes to remedy the propertylessness of the workers undermines property; he is a subversive, an enemy of society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To be sure, pity and fear continue to work — for poverty is dangerous to all of society; it breeds epidemics and crime — in bourgeois circles in favour of the proletariat and dispose many of the more deeply thinking and better feeling bourgeois to do something for the proletarians. But for the great mass of these bourgeois, who neither dare nor are able to break with their class, the problem can no longer read: <em>abolition of the proletariat,</em> but only: <em>elevation of the proletarians.</em> These are to remain fit for work and become content — but they must not cease to be compliant proletarians. Beyond this boundary bourgeois philanthropy (love of mankind) no longer proceeds.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Within these boundaries philanthropy can of course occupy itself in the most manifold ways. Most of its methods are either wholly useless or at most capable of procuring a passing relief for individual persons. But when, in the first decades of our century, large-scale capitalist industry made its entry in England (at first in the textile industry) with all the terrors it is capable of calling forth, then the most perceptive among the philanthropists arrived at the conviction that only <em>one</em> thing was capable of counteracting the complete degradation of the workers of this industry: a <em>state protection of labour</em> at least for the most defenceless strata of workers, the children and the women.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalists of large-scale industry did not at that time yet form so decisive a part of the propertied classes as today. In favour of restricting their power over the workers spoke various economic as well as political interests of the non-capitalists among the propertied classes — landowners and petty bourgeois; there spoke further in favour of it the recognition that without this restriction the foundation of England's industrial flourishing, its working class, would perish, a consideration which had to win over every perceptive member of the ruling classes who was above momentary interests to the cause of labour protection; there spoke, finally, in favour of it even the special interests of individual large manufacturers themselves, who possessed the means to bear these restrictions easily and to adapt production to them, whereas those of their smaller competitors, who only kept their heads above water with difficulty through the most atrocious grinding of their workers, were driven toward ruin by labour protection. Despite this, and although within the working class itself a powerful movement for labour protection developed, it cost hard struggles to obtain even the first timid protective laws and then to build them out ever further.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Nevertheless, however slight the achievements attained were at first, they already constituted, for the proletarian strata whom they benefited, an impulse that awakened them from their dullness and unleashed within them the elevating tendencies of their social position. Indeed, even before any victory in the matter had been won, the very struggles that were fought over it sufficed to show the proletarians how important, how necessary they were, that they signified a power. Already these struggles roused them, lent them self-consciousness and self-respect, killed off their hopelessness and gave them a goal of their striving reaching beyond what lay nearest at hand.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Another of those means for the elevation of the working class which are also supported from the bourgeois side is the <em>public elementary school.</em> A closer treatment of this, however, does not fall within the frame of the present work. It is an important means, whose significance may not be underestimated, but for the elevation of the proletariat as a <em>class</em> it is nonetheless less effective than a resolute labour protection legislation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more the capitalist mode of production develops, the more large-scale enterprise displaces the other forms of production or alters them in their essence, the more necessary becomes a steady intensification of labour protection and a steady extension of it not merely over all branches of large-scale enterprise, but also over the handicrafts and the domestic industry, as well as agriculture. But to the same degree there also grows the influence of the industrial capitalists in bourgeois society; the non-capitalist propertied classes, petty bourgeois and landowners, likewise become infected with a capitalist mentality, and the thinkers and statesmen of the bourgeoisie are transformed from its foresighted leaders into its hired bruisers, ready to stand up for each of its momentary interests.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The devastations which capitalist production wreaks among its workers are so ghastly that only the greediest and most shameless capitalists and friends of capital dare to refuse a certain, meagre measure of legal labour protection. But for an ample labour protection that goes beyond this small measure, as for example for the eight-hour day, which today means the same thing as the ten-hour day did in the forties for English industry, there are at present only few advocates to be found in the ranks of the propertied. Bourgeois philanthropy becomes ever more timid. It leaves it more and more to the workers alone to wage the struggle for adequate labour protection. The present struggle for the eight-hour day in England has quite a different aspect from the struggle that was waged there half a century ago for the ten-hour day. In so far as bourgeois politicians stand up for it today, they do so not out of philanthropy, but because they are pressed by the workers, their voters. The struggle for labour protection becomes ever more a pure <em>class struggle between proletariat and bourgeoisie.</em> On the continent of Europe, where the struggle for labour protection laws began much later than in England, it has borne this character from the very outset. The proletariat has no more support to expect from the propertied classes in its striving for social elevation. It is entirely dependent on its own strength, that is to say, in the first instance on the strength of those of its many strata which have preserved or newly won the capacity and the joy for struggle.<br/> <a id="ch5-t8" name="ch5-t8"> </a></p>
```

## 8. The Trade Union Movement

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Struggles between wage labourers and their exploiters are nothing new. We already find them at the close of the Middle Ages in the <em>handicrafts,</em> between journeymen and masters, as soon as the latter, under the influence of the unfolding of commodity production and world commerce then taking place, began to feel capitalist inklings and inclinations. As early as the fifteenth century, many a one of them tried to increase the number of journeymen he employed to such an extent that he could live off their labour without working himself, or he at least tried to shift the lion's share of the work onto the journeymen. Endeavours towards increasing the number of working days, towards curtailing the holidays, indeed even towards Sunday work, became noticeable. In doing so, the master gentlemen sought to set themselves apart from the journeymen; the latter were to make do with poorer fare, and so forth. The familial bond was loosened. Finally, the guild masters began to close themselves off; for journeymen who were not the sons or sons-in-law of masters, becoming a master was made very difficult, often downright impossible. Thus the journeyman estate became more and more an estate of its own; it ceased to be merely an intermediate station between apprenticeship and mastership.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>When the masters began to play the capitalist, the naturally necessary consequence of this was that the relationship between them and their workers took on something of the sharpness of the later antagonism between the capitalist entrepreneur and the wage proletarian. But the journeymen were not to be compared with the humble, downtrodden proletarians of the incipient large-scale industry. Defiant and eager to fight, they not only parried every blow directed against them, but if possible answered it with a still coarser blow of their own. The towns were small, and the number of journeymen of each trade in a town was therefore relatively small. They were all the easier to unite because as a rule every trade was carried on in a particular street. <em>Labour,</em> to be sure, set the one apart from the other; only a few, seldom more than one or two, worked for a single master. But labour did not fill up their lives. The number of holidays in the year was legion, and <em>sociability</em> at that time played just as great a role in the life of each individual as labour did, and sociability united the journeymen. Their taverns became the centres of their organisations, the starting points of the battles they delivered against the masters. Whoever among the journeymen did not join in was ostracised. In view of the closing-off of the trades from one another, exclusion from the journeymen's association of his handicraft was, for the journeyman, tantamount to exclusion from society. The journeymen's organisation of a trade therefore embraced <em>all</em> journeymen of it. An industrial reserve army remained as good as unknown; to draw in workers from other trades was, for the most various reasons, impossible; no wonder that the position of the journeymen vis-à-vis the masters was a comparatively most favourable one. The weapons they employed were the stoppage of work and the declaration of boycott – strike and boycott – and these weapons were not spared. Our guild enthusiasts, who dream of the restoration of the medieval handicraft and who thereby hope for the establishment of peace between the workers and their exploiters, would have their hair stand on end if today, in proportion to the extent of industry, strikes were as frequent and as stubborn as in the leading handicrafts during the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only the rising modern state power was able to teach the journeymen manners. Holding down the workers was one of the first labours of love it rendered to the bourgeoisie, and it has remained one of its principal tasks to this day. There began the age of wage schedules (that is, maximum wages) and of the prohibition, or at least the police suppression, of all organisations of wage labourers. Nevertheless, the state too did not succeed in wholly getting the better of the journeymen. Just as well as their opponents, they too knew what power they would possess through the combination of their forces, and how defenceless they would be without organisation. They everywhere tried most tenaciously to hold fast to it. Where public organisations were made impossible for them, they founded secret ones. The most terrible punishments and abuses were inflicted upon them on this account, but were unable to loosen their cohesion. The sufferings and privations that the Anti-Socialist Law brought upon the German proletariat, although in themselves bad enough, were child's play compared with what the workers had to endure during the last centuries and, in some countries, right into the middle of the present one. And yet they overcame all persecutions victoriously.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The majority of the workers of the emerging capitalist <em>manufacture</em> did not possess the same power of resistance as the journeymen of the handicraft. The operations necessary for the production of an entire product are, as we have already remarked, apportioned in manufacture among various workers, each of whom has only one or several comparatively simple manipulations to perform. The apprenticeship period of the worker is thereby shortened, and the labour of women and children already begins to penetrate. Furthermore, under the rule of manufacture we find great armies of workers in the industrial towns. There it is impossible for one worker to know another personally, something which in the medieval small towns had come about of itself among the journeymen. From the development of the large towns the handicraft journeymen too suffered in part, to be sure, but not so severely, for to the same extent as they increased in number, so also grew the multiplicity, and thereby the disunity, of their opponents, the masters. In the capitalist enterprises, on the other hand, numerous workers stand opposed to a few entrepreneurs, who can easily come to an understanding among themselves.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>There was the additional fact that the rule of guild compulsion hindered the development of manufactures in the old towns. These enterprises had to be established outside the domain of guild compulsion, mostly in the open countryside, where the workers were easier to supervise, found no backing in other strata of the working population, and remained dependent solely upon the employment opportunity of the manufacture.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Finally, however, the time for sociable gatherings – that important means of combination and of the unity of action arising therefrom – was greatly curtailed for the workers, notably through the abolition of the medieval holidays.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To be sure, manufacture brings together larger masses of workers at labour and compels them to work together, to cooperate. But the beneficial consequences that result therefrom for the cohesion of the workers are in part offset by the fact that not only were the workers recruited from the most various strata of the population, but also that the various operations are very differently remunerated; we find here a series of ranks among the workers, as with the servants of a great luxury household. To be sure, among the workers the belonging to each particular rank is determined chiefly by their achievements in production, not by their personal pliability; the hierarchy therefore does not engender among them the qualities of servility, but it nonetheless produces such great differences in the interests of the individual groups of workers of one enterprise that the community of their interests only with difficulty comes to their consciousness.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>At all events, the workers of the manufactory still possess one great advantage: although their period of apprenticeship is much shorter than in the handicraft, their work nonetheless rests upon a manual dexterity, a skill, that can be acquired only through longer practice. They are therefore not easily replaced. And however large the number of unemployed proletarians seeking work may already be at the stage of development with which we are here dealing, the number of trained manufactory workers among them is still small. The industrial reserve army has, in general, still little significance for the workers of the manufactory period.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only the machine changes this; it makes the entire mass of the unemployed serviceable to industry and throws the wives and children of the proletarians onto the labour market in droves as well. With what results for the workers' capacity for resistance, we have seen.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Since the introduction of machinery into production, the process of transforming the whole of industry into a capitalist one has taken on an exceedingly rapid pace. But the capitalist enterprises do not immediately become factories producing with machine power in all fields of production. The manufactory has in some fields, for example in typesetting, maintained itself to the present day. There are even branches of industry in which, even under capitalist operation, handicraft production can maintain itself for a time, for example in tailoring, insofar as it does not serve mass production. As a rule, however, the capitalist exploitation of a trade that still stands at the stage of the handicraft leads to the dwarf-like, domestic-industrial enterprise, not to the large-scale enterprise. But the workers of the domestic industry are the most incapable of resistance of all.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Even under the rule of large-scale industry operated with machines, there is maintained in the capitalist mode of production a series of branches of industry – gradually dwindling, to be sure – that cannot dispense with trained workers who have acquired a certain skill. Large-scale industry itself creates a series of new branches of work or extends already existing ones which presuppose a special strength or skill or a special knowledge and which have not to fear the competition of untrained workers or that of women and children. This held true, for example, and still holds true for the most part in some branches of the extraction and processing of metals.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The working proletariat accordingly divides into two great strata: a higher one, favoured by circumstances in various respects, that of the trained or skilled (English <em>skilled)</em> or <em>qualified</em> workers. Beneath this stratum spreads the large and daily increasing mass of workers who are engaged in occupations for the learning of which no special prior knowledge or skills or aptitudes are required: however skilful or knowledgeable or capable some among these workers may be, they fall into the category of untrained, unskilled <em>(unskilled)</em> or <em>unqualified</em> workers, who are easily replaceable, to whom one owes no consideration, whose capacity for resistance is slight.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is the better-placed, the qualified workers to whom the vanguard role in the struggle for the elevation of the working class falls. They form the most combative elements of the proletariat, those who are most readily able to offer resistance to capital. And they have proven their combativeness in numerous struggles.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Their position exhibits many similarities to that of the guild journeymen; the latter's traditions have in many cases also been preserved in their midst, and the latter's methods of organisation and struggle have become exemplary for them. The new economic fighting organisations of the workers – and indeed at first only of the qualified workers –, the <em>trade unions,</em> sometimes form direct continuations of the old <em>journeymen's associations;</em> often, at least in the beginnings of the trade-union movements, they have grown out of the traditions which the guild journeymanship left behind among the wage labourers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The original kinship of the trade-union movement with the guild journeymen's movement expresses itself not only in the spirit of resistance and the capacity for resistance of the trade unions. Sometimes there also comes to light in these a guild spirit, a tendency towards a caste-like exclusion, towards a one-sided pursuit merely of the narrower occupational interests without regard to the general interests of the workers. Under certain circumstances this can go so far that trade unions of qualified workers not only disregard all duties of solidarity with the whole working class, but seek directly to gain advantages at the expense of the rest of the workers – for example, by restricting the number of apprentices who are trained in their occupation. By this means they do indeed reduce the supply of labour-power in their own occupation, but only at the expense of the workers in other branches of work, who do not have the strength to enforce such restrictions, so that all the more labour-power now turns to these occupations.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Sometimes it is merely individual trades whose organised members set themselves apart as "aristocrats" of labour from the "rabble" and seek to climb higher on the latter's shoulders. This held true, for example, in Germany until some time ago for the majority of the typesetters. In England, however, the qualified workers have set themselves apart in their entirety from the unqualified. To them were added the workers of the branches of work subject to the Factory Act, who had been placed by it in a more favourable position. These better-placed workers until recently formed – and in part still form today – a labour aristocracy set apart from the great mass of the wage proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Where the trade-union movement leads to a cultivation of one-sided caste spirit and to an aristocratic exclusion of the better-placed workers, there it not only contributes nothing to the elevation of the whole proletariat as a <em>class,</em> it is even capable of <em>hindering</em> and <em>delaying</em> it. It is a far more effective means to this end than the brutal and witless measures of suppression which conventional statecraft is so fond of employing. The latter's measures directed against the fighting organisations of the working class are, on the contrary, the most effective means of welding the qualified workers together with the unqualified into a unified resistance against the suppression.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is therefore today only the most witless and most ignorant statesmen who imagine that the proletariat can be held down by such measures. The most dangerous enemies of the proletariat, on the other hand, are those who, appearing not as its opponents but as its friends, seek by means of a trade-union movement in the spirit just described to split the proletariat and to transform its most resistant components from vanguard fighters into oppressors of its more defenceless components. These false friends of the working class carry on their mischief in Germany too; admittedly, so far mostly only in the universities. But they are making attempts to gain influence over the workers themselves as well. Fortunately, the ruling parties are too narrow-minded, the German workers too clear-sighted, and the economic conditions too far developed, for these gentlemen to be able to do lasting harm.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>However greatly a stratum of workers favoured by circumstances may become arrogant and shut itself off from the mass of the proletariat, in the long run it cannot escape the effects of economic development, which press it towards union with the entire working class. Depending on the insight of this stratum of workers, on the level of economic development of its trade, and on the role which it plays in the domestic market and the world market, it may take a shorter or a longer time until its aristocratic tendencies are broken; but sooner or later it comes to this with every one of the strata of workers under discussion.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>No trade, on the other hand, is safe from being seized one day by the technical revolution, which replaces the skilled worker with the unskilled one and creates competition for the man from woman and child. Despite all restrictions on the number of apprentices and so forth, the number of the unemployed rises in every occupation, however high the demands it may make upon its workers. There rises the number of those skilled workers who, because they do not earn enough, must stand outside the organisations and can be used against the organised workers. More and more, even the most tightly organised workers with the best-filled treasuries must recognise that resistance to the depressing effects of capitalism, let alone the overcoming of them, is a task to which the isolated trade organisations are not equal. They must recognise that they are the weaker the weaker the entire proletariat is, and the stronger the stronger the latter is. They must come to the insight that it is bad policy to want to rise up on the shoulders of people who are sinking in a swamp and whom one presses still deeper into it by rising above them. They must strive to get firm ground under their feet if they wish to rise up and hold their ground there. But they cannot do this without helping the lower strata standing beneath them to raise themselves out of the swamp.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus one of the "aristocratic" strata of workers after another comes to regard its struggles against exploitation not as special struggles concerned merely with its special interests, but as parts of the great class struggle which the entire proletariat wages. They come to the recognition that the struggles of the other proletarian strata are by no means without significance for them, that in these too their own cause is at stake, and that they therefore have the duty, wherever they can, to take part in them helpfully and supportively. They come to the recognition that they must, where possible, also safeguard the interests of those proletarian strata which are not yet able to defend their own skins by their own strength, which still stand outside the framework of the labour movement.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But at the same time, one stratum after another also rises up from the ranks of the unskilled workers. The mere sight of the great struggles of the skilled workers has a rousing and encouraging effect on many of their "unskilled" brothers. We have already mentioned the similar effect exercised by the struggles for labour protection. A whole series of other causes is also at work, now and then, as the result of a favourable coincidence, in prompting a stratum of workers to enter the ranks of the fighting proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The direct economic successes of the struggles of the unskilled proletarians are as a rule slight. Their history is "a long series of defeats [of the workers], interrupted by a few isolated victories" (Engels). But like the giant Antaeus of Greek legend, the proletarians draw new strength even from defeat. Whatever the outcome of the struggle may be, it is the struggle itself that raises the workers morally, that brings out and gives effect in them to all those qualities which we have above designated as the characteristic ones of the proletariat, that promotes its moral and social rebirth, even when it contributes nothing to its economic elevation, perhaps even results in an economic worsening of its position.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus there gradually forms out of skilled and unskilled proletarians the stratum of the working class that is in motion – the labour movement. This is the part of the proletariat that fights for the general interests of its class, its <em>ecclesia militans</em> (fighting church). This stratum grows just as much at the expense of the arrogant "aristocrats" of labour, sunk in a narrow egoism, as at the expense of the dull-witted "mob," the lower strata of the wage proletariat still vegetating in hopelessness and powerlessness. We have seen that the working proletariat is in constant increase; we know further that it becomes ever more determining for the other working classes, whose living conditions, whose feeling and thinking are ever more influenced by its own; now we see that within this ever-growing mass the militant part becomes ever larger, not only in itself but also relatively. However rapidly the proletariat increases, its <em>fighting part</em> is increasing still more rapidly.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>The fighting proletariat is, however, by far the most important and most productive recruiting ground of the Social Democracy.</em> The latter is essentially nothing other than the purposeful part of the fighting proletariat; the latter has the tendency to become ever more identical with the Social Democracy; in Germany and Austria the two have in fact become one.<br/> <a id="ch5-t9" name="ch5-t9"> </a></p>
```

## 9. The Political Struggle

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Just as the proletariat models its most primitive organizations of resistance on those of the journeymen artisans, so too its most primitive means of struggle are, everywhere it appears in closed masses, the same as those the journeymen artisans employed: the <em>boycott,</em> but above all the <em>strike.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The proletariat cannot, however, stop at these means of struggle. The more the individual strata of which it consists join together into a unified working class, the more its struggles must take on a <em>political character,</em> for, as the <strong>Communist Manifesto</strong> already says, every class struggle is a political struggle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The needs of the purely trade-union struggle alone compel the workers to raise political demands. We have seen how the modern state regards it as its chief task, with respect to the wage labourers, to make their organizing impossible. But the secret organization can always be only an inadequate surrogate for the public one, and this holds all the more, the greater the masses that are to be gathered together into a single body. The more the proletariat develops, the more it needs freedom of association, the <em>freedom of coalition.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But this freedom alone does not suffice if the proletariat is to be placed in a position to build up its organizations as completely as possible and to apply them as appropriately as possible. We have already pointed out how easy it was for the journeymen artisans to come together. In every town relatively so few worked in each trade, and moreover mostly crowded together in a single street, that they were in constant personal contact with one another. A single tavern usually sufficed to accommodate the journeymen of a trade. Each town, however, formed more or less an economic whole in itself. The roads were bad, the traffic from town to town slight. Under these circumstances the individual wandering journeymen were entirely sufficient to maintain the connection between the journeymen's organizations of the various towns.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Today, in the great industrial centres, thousands of workers labour, each of whom stands in closer personal contact only with a few fellow workers, but not with the great mass of his comrades. To bring these masses into connection with one another, to awaken in them the consciousness of their broader community of interests and to win them for the organizations that serve the protection of their interests, requires the possibility of being able to speak freely to great masses; it requires the freedom of <em>assembly</em> and of the <em>press.</em> The journeymen artisans had no need of the press. For the small conditions in which they lived, oral communication sufficed. To unite the enormous masses of today's wage labourers in organizations and to bring them to unified action is downright impossible without the aid of the press.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This holds all the more, the more the modern means of communication develop. These form a powerful weapon for the capitalists in their struggles with the workers. They enable them, for example, to dispatch great masses of workers rapidly over long distances; if they come into conflict with their workers, they can easily replace them with outside labour-power, provided the latter stand in no connection with the former. The development of communications therefore makes it ever more necessary to unite the individual local movements of the workers of the various trades into a single movement encompassing the whole fighting working class of the whole country, and finally of all industrial countries.
```
