But this national and international unification of the wage-labouring class needs, even more than the local work of organization, the <em>press.</em></p>

```{=html}
<p>Thus we see everywhere that where the working class stirs, where it makes the first attempts to achieve an improvement of its economic situation, it raises, alongside purely economic demands, political ones as well, namely the demands for freedom of association, of assembly, of the press. These freedoms are of the greatest importance for the working class; they belong to its <em>conditions of life,</em> which it absolutely requires for its development. They mean light and air for the proletariat, and whoever curtails or withholds them from it, or wishes to hold the workers back from the struggle for the winning and extension of these freedoms, belongs among the worst enemies of the proletariat, however great a love for it he may feel or feign, whether he calls himself an Anarchist or a Christian-Social or whatever else. Just like its open enemies, he too harms the proletariat, and whether he does this out of malice or merely out of ignorance is a matter of indifference; he must be combated just as much as the acknowledged opponents of the proletariat.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The political struggle has sometimes been set in opposition to the economic one, and it has been declared necessary that the proletariat turn one-sidedly only to the one or the other. In truth the two cannot be separated from one another. The economic struggle requires the political rights just named, which, however, do not fall from heaven, but require for their attainment and assertion the most energetic political activity. The political struggle itself, however, is in the last instance also an economic struggle; often it is downright directly such a one, for example in matters of tariffs, worker protection, and the like. The political struggle is only a particular form of the economic struggle, the most comprehensive and usually also the most incisive one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Not only the laws that directly concern the working class in particular, but also the great majority of the others touch its interests more or less. Like every other class, therefore, the working class too must strive after political influence and political power, must strive to make the state power serviceable to itself.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For this there are in the <em>modern</em> state two ways: first, the personal influencing of the head of state. This was (and is) in the absolutistically governed states the only possibility of acting upon the administration of the state. It lies in the interest of those classes which have personal access to the head of state and are in a position to acquire his favour, to make him dependent upon themselves or to prove themselves useful to him. These classes—the court nobility, the higher clergy, the heads of the army and of the bureaucracy, and finally the great creditors, the gentlemen of high finance—are therefore the natural defenders of the absolute form of government.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>All the other classes of the population can, in a modern great state, gain influence over the administration of the state only by means of a <em>parliament</em> elected by them, a parliament that lays down the conditions under which the classes it represents are prepared to grant the necessary contributions of the population for the state budget. The right and the possibility of <em>refusing taxes</em> is the foundation out of which the rights of making or rejecting laws and of overthrowing ministries have developed, rights that belong to every parliament which really deserves this name, which is more than what has been called a fig-leaf of absolutism.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We may leave direct legislation by the people out of consideration here. At least in a modern great state — and it is only with such that we are here concerned — it cannot render parliament superfluous; at most it can come into operation alongside parliament in individual cases to correct it. To have it manage the entire legislation of the state is absolutely impossible, and just as impossible is it to supervise the state administration through it and, if necessary, to direct it. As long as the modern great state exists, the centre of gravity of political activity will always lie in its parliament.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The ultimate consequence of parliamentarism is the parliamentary republic; whether this still retains the monarchy as a decoration, as the English do, or not, is fairly immaterial. In fact, in a truly parliamentary-governed country the state administration falls into complete dependence on the parliament, which holds the money-bag, that is, the soul of the modern state as of every modern enterprise, in its hand. And a king without money is today even worse off than a king without land.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The striving of all classes that develop an independent, vigorous political life and that have no prospect of reaching their goal more quickly through personal influence upon the head of state is directed, in the modern state, on the one hand towards increasing the power of parliament and on the other towards increasing their power within parliament. The power of parliament depends on the strength and courage of the classes that stand behind it, and on the strength and courage of the classes upon which it has to impose its will. The power of a class within parliament depends first of all on the shape of the electoral law. Classes that possess no franchise cannot, of course, be represented in parliament. But then it depends on the power and influence which this class exercises over the circles of voters, and finally it depends on the aptitude of the class in question for parliamentary activity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The first points require no elaboration; only to the last shall a few words be devoted. Parliamentary activity is not everybody's affair. It presupposes a certain adroitness, which is acquired only through a longer, especially oratorical, effectiveness in public life. It further requires a wider horizon, an eye capable of grasping questions of general national and international significance. The peasant population, and likewise the greater part of the petty-bourgeois population, lacks these preconditions for participation in parliamentary life. We have seen that gainful labour claims these circles entirely. But that labour isolates them, separates one from another, restricts the intercourse of each to a small circle. Their horizon is necessarily an exceedingly restricted one; even questions of the most general significance they measure by the yardstick of personal, or at most local, momentary needs. Their living conditions not only hinder the development of parliamentary politicians among them, they also hinder these classes from combining into firm national (in the sense of embracing the class throughout the whole nation) parties. They form incoherent masses, ruled by momentary moods; they are not only incapable of sending representatives from their own class into parliament, they are also incapable of keeping the men of their choice under sharp control. A handicraftsman, or even a genuine peasant, in a parliament is a white raven. If the peasants and petty bourgeois want to be represented in parliament, then they elect not one of their own kind but an advocate or professor if they are liberal, a great landowner, cleric or higher official if they are conservative. That such a deputy, even if he has honest intentions, does not constitute the most fitting representative of the interests of the handicraftsmen and peasants is clear. But very often he has no honest intentions at all. Once in parliament, he can do as he pleases; his electors lack all means of influencing him. At most they can let the traitor fall through at the next election — in order to elect another traitor.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>No wonder that peasants and petty bourgeois are ill-disposed towards parliamentarism. It stands quite otherwise with the bourgeoisie. It has at its disposal all the material and intellectual means to safeguard its interests both in the electoral contests and in the struggles within parliament itself. It commands not only sufficient monetary resources, it also finds in its ranks numerous educated men, men with knowledge of the world, men who are accustomed to administer great social organizations, men who have made a profession out of knowledge of the law, indeed out of speaking: advocates and professors. No other class has hitherto equalled it in this; it has therefore dominated the parliaments right up to the most recent times, and parliamentarism has shown itself to be the means most in accordance with its nature and most effective for securing to it the domination in the state and for making the strength of the lower classes politically serviceable to it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The radical petty bourgeois who would like to put an end to capitalism is therefore inclined to see in parliamentarism the chief cause that maintains and perpetuates the bondage of the lower classes. He will have nothing to do with it and is convinced that only by a complete turning away from parliamentarism can the overthrow of the bourgeoisie be brought about. The one party demands the complete replacement of parliament by direct legislation, the others go still further; since they recognize that in the modern state politics and parliamentarism are inseparably bound together, they condemn all political activity whatsoever. That may sound very revolutionary, but is in fact nothing but a political declaration of bankruptcy on the part of the lower classes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The proletariat stands in a more favourable position towards parliamentarism than peasants and petty bourgeois. We have already seen how the modern mode of production raises the proletarian above philistinism, how it awakens in him a thirst for knowledge and an understanding of the totality of phenomena, of great questions of general significance. By this alone he is superior as a politician to the petty bourgeois and peasant. He is more readily capable of grasping party principles, he inclines to a principled politics, uninfluenced by momentary moods, personal or local interests. But the conditions of life in which he finds himself also compel him to unite with his comrades in great masses, to act uniformly with them. His conditions of life produce in him a strict discipline, which, however, he demands of his own champions just as it is demanded of him. And activity on behalf of the proletarian organizations, as well as activity within them, is an excellent parliamentary school; it accustoms to parliamentary forms, trains speakers, men versed in the law, and organizers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The proletariat is therefore capable — and it has already proved it, as one knows best in Germany — of forming a party of its own, independent of the other classes, which neither peasants nor petty bourgeois have hitherto succeeded in doing, and which they are also unlikely ever to succeed in doing anywhere else; it also knows very well how to control its representatives and to keep them serviceable to its interests <a href="#ch5-n11" id="ch5-f11" name="ch5-f11">[11]</a>; finally, it finds ever more people in its own ranks who are capable of representing it successfully as deputies in parliament.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Where the proletariat, as a self-conscious class, takes part in the struggles over parliament (particularly the electoral struggles) and within parliament, parliamentarism too begins to change its former character. It ceases to be a mere instrument of domination of the bourgeoisie. It is precisely these struggles which prove to be the most powerful means of rousing the still indifferent strata of the proletariat, of instilling in them confidence and joyful hope; they prove to be the most powerful means of welding the various strata of the proletariat ever more firmly into a unified working class, and finally also the most powerful means presently at the disposal of the proletariat for influencing the state power in its favour and for wringing from it those concessions which, according to the circumstances, can for the time being be wrung from it at all: in short, these struggles belong among the most effective levers for raising the proletariat out of its economic, social and moral degradation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The working class therefore not only has no reason to keep away from parliamentarism, it has every reason to be active in the most decisive manner everywhere for the strengthening of parliament against the state administration and for the strengthening of its representation in parliament. Alongside the <em>right of combination</em> and <em>freedom of the press</em>, <em>universal suffrage</em> constitutes a <em>condition of life</em> for the flourishing development of the proletariat.<br/> <a id="ch5-t10" name="ch5-t10"> </a></p>
```

## 10. The Workers' Party

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Not in all countries does the working class already possess these conditions of life; almost nowhere does it possess them to a sufficient degree; almost everywhere there exists the tendency to curtail again what has already been won by it. It requires long and sacrificial struggles for the working class to gain and to assert the necessary political rights.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the beginnings of its political struggles the proletariat's task is sometimes made easier by struggles within the propertied classes themselves. The industrial capitalists, the merchants, the landowners, the courtly, absolutist classes, and so forth, very often fall into fierce discord with one another. Under these circumstances each of them seeks allies, whom it tries to induce, by means of a small concession, to attach themselves to it. Often, after victory has been won, the ally is then cheated out of its share of the spoils; on the other hand, a political party has also often seen itself moved to grant one of the lower classes a significant political right, in order thereby to enable it to render useful services to that party.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The ruling parties have often enough appealed to the proletariat; they have themselves dragged it into the arena of political struggle. As long as it had <em>not</em> yet attained an independent politics, they regarded it as mere "voting cattle," which, like the peasants and petty bourgeois, could be exploited to render heerbann service to its own exploiters. And indeed, often enough it has performed this service.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Meanwhile, the interests of the proletariat and those of the bourgeoisie are of too antagonistic a nature for the political endeavours of the two classes to be able to be united with one another in the long run. Sooner or later, in every country of the capitalist mode of production, the participation of the working class in politics must lead to its detaching itself from the bourgeois parties and forming an independent party, the <em>Workers' Party.</em> This lies in the nature of the matter and, after our expositions concerning the interests, endeavours and views of the two classes, requires no further elucidation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>When the proletariat in a country comes to take this decisive step, to cut, as it were, the umbilical cord that politically links it to bourgeois society, from whose womb it has emerged—this depends above all on the level of economic development of the country in question, by which indeed the extent, strength and cohesion of the proletariat are chiefly determined. But there is also a series of other conditions which are of significance for the earlier or later occurrence of the political independence of the working class. Two in particular are to be named here: the level of the working class's insight into political and economic conditions, and the conduct of the bourgeois parties towards it. Both conditions have hitherto in Germany been particularly favourable to the political detachment of the workers from the bourgeoisie, much more favourable than in any other of the great industrial states; and so it has come about that in Germany the labour movement, with regard to independence from the bourgeois parties, is far ahead of the labour movements of other countries.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But however different, under the influence of the various conditions, the moment may be at which the labour movement of the individual countries of the capitalist mode of production condenses into a separate workers' party, in each of these countries economic development brings it about with natural necessity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Every political party, however, must set political rule as its goal. It must strive to make the state power subservient to itself, that is, to the interests of the class it represents; it must strive to become the ruling party in the state. With the fact that the working class organises itself as an independent party, this goal is given to it with natural necessity, and just as naturally does economic development bring about the attainment of that goal. Here too, as with the detachment of the workers from the bourgeois parties, the <em>moment</em> of the occurrence of this event depends not on the level of the country's industrial development alone, but alongside this a series of other conditions of a national and international nature act as co-determinants. The <em>manner</em> too in which it takes place can be very different. But what can be doubtful to no one who has followed the economic and political development of modern society, especially in the course of this century, is <em>the natural necessity of the final victory of the proletariat.</em> While it increases ever more in extent, in moral and political strength and in economic indispensability, while the class struggle educates it to solidarity and discipline and widens its horizon, while its organisations steadily grow in scope and cohesion, while in the domain of the capitalist mode of production it becomes ever more the most important and finally the sole working class, upon whose activity the whole of society is dependent, the classes hostile to the proletariat melt away ever more in number, lose visibly in moral and political strength, and become not only superfluous but in part downright obstructive to the progress of production, which under their direction falls into ever more hopeless confusion and conjures up ever more intolerable conditions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In view of this, there is no doubt as to which side victory will finally incline. Long ago the propertied classes were seized with dread at their approaching end. They will not admit to themselves the terribleness of their situation; they seek to lie it away and to shout it away in jubilation; they blind themselves in order not to see the abyss towards which they are hastening, and do not notice that through their self-delusion they accelerate their downfall and make it still more disastrous.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The proletariat, as the lowest of the exploited classes—the lumpenproletariat is not exploited; it itself belongs to the parasites—cannot, however, use the rule it has conquered, as other classes before it have done, to shift the burden of exploitation onto other shoulders, to make itself into an exploiting class. It must use it to put an end to its own exploitation, and thereby to all exploitation in bourgeois society. The root of its exploitation, however, is private property in the means of production. The proletariat can only remove the former if it abolishes the latter. If its <em>propertylessness</em> makes it <em>possible</em> to win the proletariat over to the abolition of this private property, then its <em>exploitation compels</em> the proletariat to carry out this abolition and to put cooperative production in the place of capitalist production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But we have seen that this cannot become the general form of production under the dominion of commodity production. In order to put cooperative production generally in place of capitalist production, it is indispensable that at the same time production for the community and under the control of the community takes the place of production for the market — of commodity production. <em>Socialist production</em> is thus the naturally necessary consequence of the victory of the proletariat. Should the proletariat not consciously wish to use its dominion in the state to seize the means of production by means of the state power and to put socialist production in place of capitalist commodity production, then the logic of the facts would ultimately call this into being, though perhaps only after various blunders, errors and superfluous sacrifices, after a useless squandering of energy and time. But socialist production will and must come. Its victory is inevitable as soon as that of the proletariat has become inevitable. The proletariat <em>must</em> strive to use its victory for the removal of its exploitation, and it <em>cannot</em> reach this goal in any way other than through socialist production. Economic and political development itself offers the points of connection for this in the large-scale enterprises, the cartels, the state economy; it will itself press the proletariat in the direction of socialism, it will cause any possible attempt of the victorious proletariat of a country to move in another direction to fail, and so this proletariat will ultimately tread the path of socialism even if it should at first be averse to it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is, however, by no means to be expected that the proletariat of any country should adopt such a rejecting attitude once it has come to power. That would mean nothing other than that it remained at the childhood stage in its consciousness and its knowledge, while it matured economically, politically and morally into the man who possesses the strength and the ability to overcome his powerful opponents and to force his will upon them. Such a disproportion in development is, however, in the case of the proletariat precisely most improbable. We have already pointed out several times how, thanks to the machine, there grows in the proletariat, as soon as it has once raised itself out of its initial degradation, a theoretical sense, a receptivity for great problems and goals lying beyond the sphere of momentary interests, such as one would search for in vain among the other working and gainfully occupied classes before it and above it. At the same time, however, the economic development of present-day society proceeds so rapidly and expresses itself in such striking mass phenomena that it becomes recognisable even to the unlearned as soon as he has once been pointed to it. And this pointing is not lacking, for at the same time the insight into the course of economic development and the whole economic mechanism has become an exceedingly deep- and far-reaching one, thanks to the continuation by <em>Karl Marx</em> of the work begun by bourgeois classical economics.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Everything combines to make the fighting proletariat receptive in the highest degree to the socialist doctrines. Socialism is for it no message of misfortune, but a glad tidings, a new gospel. The ruling classes cannot recognise socialism without committing moral suicide. The proletariat draws from it new life, new strength, enthusiasm and joyful hope. And should it in the long run stand indifferent or even rejecting towards such a doctrine?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Where it comes to the formation of an independent workers' party, this party must with natural necessity sooner or later adopt socialist tendencies, if it is not filled with such from the outset; it must ultimately become a <em>socialist</em> workers' party: <em>Social Democracy.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We now see its principal recruiting ground precisely delimited before us. Briefly put, the result of our latest expositions shows the following: it is the fighting strata of the industrial proletariat, which have attained political self-consciousness, that form the bearers of the socialist movement. But the more the influence of the proletariat grows upon the social strata neighbouring on it, the more it influences their feeling and thinking, the more these too are drawn into the socialist movement.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The class struggle of the proletariat has as its natural goal socialist production; it cannot end before this has been attained. As certainly as the proletariat must ultimately become the ruling class in the state, just as certain is the victory of socialism.<br/> <a id="ch5-t11" name="ch5-t11"> </a></p>
```

## 11. The Labour Movement and Socialism

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The socialists by no means recognised from the outset the role which the fighting proletariat is called upon to play in the socialist movement. They could not do so, of course, as long as there was no fighting proletariat. Socialism, however, is older than the class struggle of the proletariat. It is as old as the appearance of the proletariat as a mass phenomenon. But this had existed for a long time without any stirring of independent life. The first and, at that time, the only root of socialism was the <em>compassion</em> which the philanthropists of the upper classes felt for the poor and the wretched. The socialists were the boldest and most far-sighted of these friends of humanity, those who most clearly recognised the roots of the proletariat in private property in the means of production, and who did not shrink from drawing the most extreme conclusions from this recognition. Socialism was the most characterful, most penetrating and most magnificent expression of bourgeois philanthropy. There was no class interest to which the socialists could then appeal in the struggle for their aims; they could only turn to the enthusiasm and the sympathy of the idealists in the upper classes; they sought to win them over, on the one hand through alluring portrayals of a socialist community, and on the other through vivid depictions of the prevailing misery. Not through struggle, but through peaceful persuasion, were the rich and powerful to be moved to provide the means for a thorough alleviation of misery, for the establishment of the ideal society. The socialists of this period, as is well known, waited in vain for the millionaires and princes whose magnanimity was to redeem humanity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the first decades of our century the proletariat began to give signs of an independent life. In the thirties we already find a vigorous labour movement in France and especially in England.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the socialists confronted it without understanding. They did not consider it possible that the poor, ignorant, coarse proletarians could ever attain that moral height and social power which are required for carrying through the socialist aspirations. But it was not merely distrust that they felt towards the labour movement. It also became inconvenient for them, if only for the reason that it threatened to rob them of an effective argument. For the bourgeois socialists could only hope that the sensitive bourgeois would perceive the necessity of socialism if they succeeded in proving that this was the only means of stemming misery even to some degree, that every attempt at an alleviation of misery, at an elevation of the propertyless, would prove futile in modern society, and that it was impossible for the proletarians to raise themselves by their own strength. But the labour movement proceeded from premises which contradicted this train of thought. To this was added yet another factor. The class struggle between the proletariat and the bourgeoisie naturally embittered the latter against the aspiring proletarians. In the eyes of the bourgeoisie these were transformed from pitiable unfortunates who had to be helped into a depraved rabble that had to be beaten down and held down. The chief root of socialism in bourgeois circles, compassion for the poor and the wretched, began to wither. The socialist doctrines themselves no longer appeared to the alarmed bourgeoisie as a harmless plaything, but as a highly dangerous weapon which might fall into the hands of the multitude and thereby cause untold calamity. In short, the more strongly the labour movement grew, the more difficult socialist propaganda became in the ruling classes, and the more hostilely these confronted socialism.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As long as the socialists held the view that the means for attaining the socialist aims could come only from the upper classes, they were bound accordingly not only to confront the labour movement with distrust, but often even to arrive at decided hostility towards it, and to incline to the view that nothing damaged the cause of socialism more than the class struggle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The dismissive attitude of the bourgeois socialists towards the labour movement was naturally not without influence on the position of the latter towards socialism. If the aspiring part of the proletariat found among these socialists in its struggles not only no support, but rather resistance, if their doctrines threatened to discourage it instead of firing it, then distrust and aversion against the totality of the socialist doctrines, and not merely against their application to the struggles of the present, could all too easily take root in the proletariat. This was favoured by the lack of education and the thoughtlessness which prevailed among the masses even of the fighting proletarians at the beginning of the labour movement. The narrowness of their horizon made it difficult for them to grasp the ultimate aims of socialism, and they still lacked an insightful and clear consciousness of the social position and the tasks of their class; they felt only a dull <em>class instinct</em> which taught them to regard with distrust everything that came from the bourgeoisie, and hence also the socialism of that time as well as bourgeois philanthropy in general.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In some strata of workers, especially in England, the distrust of socialism took deep root at that time. Its after-effects are in part to be credited – alongside many other causes – for why England, until a decade ago, was as good as inaccessible to socialist aspirations, although the more recent socialism takes a quite different attitude towards the labour movement than that of the bourgeois utopians.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Nevertheless, however great the gulf between the fighting proletariat and socialism might at times become, the latter corresponds so much to the needs of the more far-thinking proletarian that even where the masses confronted socialism with hostility, the best minds of the working class gladly turned to it, in so far as they had the opportunity to become acquainted with its doctrines. Through them the views of the bourgeois socialists now underwent an important transformation. They did not, like the latter, have to show consideration for the bourgeoisie, which they hated and bitterly combated; the peaceful socialism of the bourgeois utopians, which wanted to bring the redemption of humanity by means of the intervention of the best elements of the upper classes, was transformed among the workers into a violent, revolutionary socialism, whose realisation proletarian fists were to see to.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But this primitive workers' socialism, too, had no understanding of the labour movement; it too stood in hostile opposition to the class struggle—at least to its highest form, the political one. Admittedly for different reasons than the bourgeois utopians. Scientifically, it was impossible for it to advance beyond the latter. The proletarian can at best appropriate a part of the knowledge that bourgeois scholarship has brought to light and process it in accordance with his purposes and needs, but he lacks—so long as he remains a proletarian—the leisure and the means to carry science independently beyond the measure attained by bourgeois thinkers. Thus the primitive workers' socialism too was bound to bear all the essential characteristics of utopianism: it had no inkling of the economic development which creates the material elements of socialist production and, through the class struggle, rears and matures that class which is called upon to seize these elements and to develop the new society out of them. Like the bourgeois utopians, these proletarians too believed that a form of society was a building which could be erected arbitrarily according to a predetermined plan, if only one had the means and the site for it. The strength to see to the construction the proletarian utopians, as energetic and bold as they were naive, already credited to themselves; the only question was to procure for them the necessary site and the necessary means. They naturally awaited no millionaire and no prince who would place these at their disposal; the <em>revolution</em> was to supply what was needed, was to tear down the old building, shatter the old powers, and confer upon the inventor or the small group of inventors of the new building plan the dictatorship which would enable the new Messiah to erect the edifice of socialist society.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In this line of thought the class struggle found no place. The proletarian utopians felt too bitterly the misery in which they lived not to wish impatiently for its immediate abolition. Even if they had considered it possible that the class struggle could gradually raise the proletariat and enable it to develop society further, this procedure would have seemed to them far too cumbersome. But they did not believe in this elevation. They stood at the beginnings of the labour movement; the strata of proletarians who took part in it were few, and among these few fighting proletarians only isolated individuals were to be found who had anything more in view than the safeguarding of their momentary interests. To educate the great mass of the population to socialist thinking seemed hopeless. The only thing this mass could accomplish was an outburst of despair, in which it destroyed the existing order and thereby cleared the path for the socialists. The worse things went for the masses, the nearer, so the primitive workers' socialists believed, must be the moment when their condition became so unbearable for them that they burst asunder the social superstructure that was crushing them. A struggle for the gradual elevation of the working class was, in the view of these socialists, not merely hopeless, it was decidedly harmful, because the slight improvements it might temporarily achieve made the existing order more bearable for the masses and thereby postponed the moment of their uprising and of the collapse of the existing order, and with it also the moment of the thorough abolition of misery. Every form of the class struggle that did not aim at the immediate and complete overthrow of the existing order, and thus every form of it that could be taken seriously and was effective, was accordingly regarded in the eyes of these socialists as nothing less than a <em>betrayal of the cause of humanity.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is more than half a century since this line of thought, which perhaps found its most brilliant expression through <em>Weitling</em>, emerged in the working class. It has not yet died out today. The inclination towards it appears in every stratum of the proletariat that is preparing to enter the ranks of the fighting proletariat; it appears in every country whose proletariat is beginning to attain consciousness of its unworthy and unbearable condition and to fill itself with socialist tendencies, without yet possessing clear insight into social conditions and without crediting itself with the strength for a sustained class struggle; and since ever new strata of the proletariat rise up out of the mire into which economic development has pressed them down, and since ever new countries fall prey to the capitalist mode of production and to proletarianisation, this line of thought of the primitive utopian workers' socialists can arise ever anew. It is a childhood disease which threatens every young proletarian-socialist movement that has not yet advanced beyond utopianism.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Nowadays this kind of socialist outlook is termed <em>anarchism,</em> but it is by no means necessarily bound up with the latter. Since it springs not from clear insight but only from instinctive rebellion against the existing order, it is compatible with the most diverse theoretical standpoints. But it is true that in more recent times the crude and violent socialism of the primitive proletarian and the often very sensitive, delicately strung and peaceable anarchism of the refined petty bourgeois are fond of joining together, since, for all their profound differences, they have one thing in common: aversion to, indeed hatred of, the sustained class struggle, in particular of its highest form, the political struggle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>As little as the bourgeois, the proletarian utopian socialism was able to overcome the opposition between socialism and the labour movement. It is true that participation in the class struggle was at times forced upon the proletarian utopians by circumstances, but given their theoretical instability, this participation did not amount to an eventual union of socialism and the labour movement, but to a displacement of the former by the latter. It is well known that the anarchist movement (the word here taken in the sense of this proletarian utopianism) has, wherever it became a mass movement, a real class struggle, always ended sooner or later, despite its apparent radicalism, in guild-minded mere trade-unionism.<br/> <a id="ch5-t12" name="ch5-t12"> </a></p>
```

## 12. Social Democracy – the Union of the Labour Movement and Socialism

```{=html}
<p class="fst">If the socialist movement and the labour movement were to be reconciled with one another and fused into a single unified movement, then socialism had to be raised above the sphere of ideas belonging to utopianism. To have accomplished this is the world-historical deed of <em>Marx</em> and <em>Engels,</em> who in 1847 laid the scientific foundations of the new, modern socialism, or, as one says today, of Social Democracy, in their <strong>Communist Manifesto</strong>. They thereby gave socialism a backbone; they made it, which until then had been a beautiful dream of a few well-meaning enthusiasts, into a serious object of struggle; they demonstrated it to be the naturally necessary consequence of economic development. To the fighting proletariat, however, they gave a clear consciousness of its historical tasks, and they enabled it to hasten toward its great goal as quickly and with as few sacrifices as possible. The socialists no longer have the task of freely <em>inventing</em> the new society, but rather of <em>discovering</em> its elements in the existing society. They no longer have to bring the proletariat its redemption from its misery from above, but rather have to support its class struggle by increasing its insight and promoting its economic and political organisations, so that it may ripen more quickly and more painlessly toward the moment when it is able to redeem itself. <em>To shape the class struggle of the proletariat as purposefully and expediently as possible, that is the task of Social Democracy.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A further exposition of the train of thought of the doctrine of Marx and Engels is not necessary, for the whole of the present book rests upon it, is nothing but a presentation and elaboration of it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The class struggle of the proletariat acquires a new character through this doctrine. As long as socialist production is not set for it as a conscious goal, as long as the strivings of the fighting proletariat do not go beyond the framework of the existing mode of production, the class struggle apparently always moves in a circle, without advancing from the spot, and the proletariat's struggle for a satisfactory existence appears as a Sisyphean labour. For the depressing tendencies of the capitalist mode of production are not eliminated by the class struggle and its achievements, but at best only somewhat pushed back. The proletarianisation of the middle strata of society proceeds uninterruptedly; incessantly, individual members and whole strata of the working classes are pushed down into the lumpenproletariat; incessantly, the profit-madness of the capitalists threatens all the achievements of the better-placed strata of the working class. Every shortening of working time, whether won through economic or political struggle, becomes the occasion for the introduction of labour-saving machinery, for the more intense exertion of the workers; every improvement of the proletarian organisations is answered by an improvement of the capitalist organisations, and so forth. And with this, unemployment grows, the crises increase in extent and depth, the insecurity of existence becomes ever greater and more tormenting. The elevation of the working class which the class struggle brings about is less an economic than a moral one. The economic conditions of the proletarians on the whole improve only little and slowly as a result of the class struggle and its achievements — if they improve at all. But the self-respect of the proletarians rises, and the respect which the other classes of society pay them; they begin to feel themselves equal to those placed higher and to compare their fate with theirs; they begin to make greater demands upon themselves, upon their dwelling and clothing, their knowledge, the education of their children, and so forth; they demand to take part in all the achievements of culture. And they become ever more sensitive to every slight and oppression.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This moral elevation of the proletariat is equivalent to the awakening and steady growth of its "covetousness." This grows much more rapidly than the improvements of its economic condition compatible with the present mode of exploitation can increase. All these improvements, of which some hope and others again fear that they would make the workers content, must lag behind the demands of the workers, which are the naturally necessary consequence of their moral elevation. The consequence of the class struggle can therefore always only be an increase in the discontent of the proletariat with its lot. A discontent which naturally makes itself felt most strongly there where the economic elevation of the proletariat lags furthest behind its moral elevation, but whose increase cannot in the long run be prevented anywhere. And thus the class struggle ultimately appears as purposeless and fruitless if it does not strive beyond the existing mode of production. The higher it raises the proletarian, the further he sees himself from the goal of his striving, a contented existence corresponding to his conceptions of human dignity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only socialist production can put an end to the disproportion between the demands of the workers and the means of satisfying them, by abolishing exploitation and class distinctions; it thereby removes that powerful incentive to the worker's discontent with his lot which today is produced by the example of the exploiters' luxury. Once this incentive is cleared out of the way, the limitation of the workers' demands to the measure compatible with the available means of satisfying their needs follows of itself. How greatly socialist production increases these means themselves, we have already seen.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Gnawing discontent, "covetousness," is unknown in communist societies. On the other hand, it arises with natural necessity out of class antagonism and exploitation there where the exploited feel themselves morally equal or even superior to the exploiters. Once an exploited class has come this far, then its "covetousness" will not be stilled until it has put an end to its exploitation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>So long, therefore, as the class struggle of the proletariat stood in opposition to socialism, so long as it aimed at nothing more than to win for the proletariat a satisfactory position by means of concessions within the framework of the present society, it was impossible for it to attain its goal. It resembled an endless screw. It is quite otherwise since the socialist movement has been fused with the labour movement: now the latter has a goal towards which it visibly draws nearer; now all sides of this struggle become significant, even those which do not directly entail practical consequences, provided only that they promote the self-consciousness and standing of the proletariat, its cooperative cohesion and its discipline. Now many an apparently lost battle becomes a victory; now every lost strike, every rejected bill that should have served the interests of the proletariat, means a step forward towards the goal of attaining a life worthy of a human being. From now on it becomes apparent that all economic and political measures taken with regard to the proletariat turn out to its advantage, whether they pursue tendencies hostile or friendly to it, whether they succeed or fail, so long only as they contribute to its awakening and moral elevation. From now on the struggling proletariat no longer resembles an army that cannot move from the spot and only with the greatest losses laboriously holds the positions it has once won; now it becomes clear, little by little, even to the dullest eye, that it is an irresistible conqueror whose triumphal march nothing can hinder.<br/> <a id="ch5-t13" name="ch5-t13"> </a></p>
```

## 13. The Internationality of Social Democracy

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The new socialism founded by Marx and Engels had its origin in Germany. Its two founders were Germans, Germans were their first pupils, and the first writings that expounded it appeared in the German language. This alone explains—though it was by no means the sole cause—why the fusion of the labour movement with socialism was first accomplished in Germany, why <em>Social Democracy</em> first took root in <em>Germany</em>—whereby Germany here is to be understood not as the German Reich, but as every region inhabited by a larger number of German-speaking workers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the spread of Social Democracy has not remained confined to Germany. The founders of modern socialism recognised from the outset the international character which the labour movement of today strives everywhere to assume, and therefore sought from the beginning to give their propaganda an international basis.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>International intercourse is necessarily bound up with the capitalist mode of production. Its development out of simple commodity production is most intimately connected with the development of world trade. This, however, is not possible without the peaceful intercourse of the individual nations with one another; for its unfolding it requires that the foreign merchant be protected in the country with which he trades just as he is in his own. Through the development of world trade the merchant is also greatly raised on the social ladder. His way of thinking begins to greatly influence the way of thinking of society as a whole. But the merchant has always been a mobile element; his principle has ever been: <em>Ubi bene, ibi patria</em>—where things go well for me, where there are profits to be had, there is my fatherland.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus, to the extent that world trade and capitalist production spread, cosmopolitan tendencies develop within bourgeois society, the desire for an eternal peace among the nations, for a fraternisation of peoples.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the capitalist mode of production generates the strangest contradictions; just as it is at once characterised by the tendencies toward the increase of equality and of inequality, toward the depression of the proletariat into the deepest degradation and toward its elevation to the ruling class, toward the complete freedom of the individual and toward his utter enslavement side by side, so too, hand in hand with its tendency toward the fraternisation of peoples goes the tendency toward the heightening of national antagonisms. Intercourse requires peace, but competition generates war. If in every country there exists a perpetual state of war among the individual capitalists and the individual classes with one another, so too there is one between the capitalists and capitalist classes of the individual nations. Every nation seeks to extend the market for its products and to drive the others from that market. The more developed world intercourse becomes, the more necessary world peace becomes, the more savage the competitive struggle, the greater the danger of hostile collisions between the individual nations. The more intimate the international intercourse, the louder the demand for national self-enclosure. The stronger the need for peace, the more threatening the danger of war: these seemingly mad contradictions correspond perfectly to the character of the capitalist mode of production. They already lie concealed within simple commodity production, but only capitalist production unfolds them into the gigantic and the intolerable. That it heightens the warlike tendencies to the same degree that it makes peace indispensable is only one of the many contradictions from which it must perish.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The proletariat takes no part in the contradictory attitude that arises from this for the other classes of present-day society. The more it develops and becomes an independent class, the more clearly there appears, in the most varied fields, the phenomenon that of two opposing tendencies of the capitalist mode of production only <em>one</em> touches it, while the other becomes ever more ineffective for it. Thus, for example, the present-day mode of production simultaneously generates the tendency toward the combination of producers in large associations for common activity and the tendency toward the bitter struggle of all (producers) against all. In the proletariat the second tendency ceases to operate. Instead of the antagonism between <em>monopoly</em> and <em>competition</em>, which wears out and rends the bourgeoisie, we find here more and more only the former tendency active in the direction of strengthening and extending proletarian <em>solidarity.</em> A natural consequence of this “one-sidedness” is that increasingly it is also only the tendency toward intimate <em>international union</em> that influences the proletarians of the various civilised countries, and that the tendency toward <em>national self-enclosure</em> and <em>national struggle</em> loses all effect in their ranks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist mode of production has, by making the worker propertyless, detached him from the soil. He no longer has a fixed home and therefore also no fixed homeland. Like the merchant, he too adopts the principle: Ubi bene, ibi patria—where the best working conditions are, there is my homeland. The journeymen artisans had already begun to extend their wanderings into foreign lands, and the beginnings of an international connection, to which we have already pointed, were the consequence of this. But what are these wanderings compared with the modern ones, such as the development of present-day traffic makes possible! And the journeyman artisan wandered with the intention of returning to his homeland; the modern proletarian wanders with wife and child in order to remain where the best working conditions are attainable for him. He is not a tourist, but a nomad.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Like the proletarian, the merchant too—at least the merchant of world trade—seeks to make himself independent of the soil and to settle where he believes he can best safeguard his business interests. But he does not thereby lose the connection with his fatherland. For his standing abroad, his position, the possibility of conducting business unhindered and of duping his foreign colleagues, depends for the most part on the importance and power of the state to which he belongs, which protects him. The merchant abroad therefore remains nationally minded; as a rule these gentlemen are the most chauvinistic fellows; they feel most immediately what the greatness of their fatherland means for their purse. It is otherwise with the proletarian. He has not been spoilt in his homeland by state protection of his interests; as a rule he does not need the protection of his fatherland abroad either, at least in the civilised countries. On the contrary, when he seeks out a foreign country, it is usually one whose laws and state administration are more favourable to the workers than those of his homeland. And his new fellow workers have no interest whatsoever in depriving him, say, of his legal protection in the very case in which he needs it most urgently, namely as against his exploiter. Their own interest rather commands them to see to it that his power of resistance against his exploiter grows.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Quite differently from the wandering journeyman artisan of the guild era and from the merchant, the modern proletarian is therefore detached from his soil. He becomes a true citizen of the world; the whole world is his homeland.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Admittedly, this world citizenship brings with it manifold disadvantages, sometimes downright dangers, for the workers of those countries in which a higher standard of living and better working conditions prevail, in which immigration therefore outweighs emigration. For it is undeniable that these higher-standing workers are hampered in their class struggle by the competition of the less demanding and less resistant immigrants.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under certain circumstances, this competition can, just like the competition among the capitalists of different nations, lead to a sharpening of national antagonisms, to national hatred of the native workers against the foreigners. But the nationality struggle, which is a permanent phenomenon in the circles of the bourgeoisie, can in the circles of the proletarians always be only of a transitory nature.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For sooner or later they must come to the recognition – if not in another way, then through bitter experience – that the immigration of cheap labour-power from backward into economically advanced regions is just as necessarily bound up with the capitalist mode of production as the introduction of machinery and the penetration of women into industry, and that it can be suppressed just as little as these two last phenomena.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In another way, the labour movement of an advanced country is harmed by the backwardness of the workers in foreign countries, in that the degree of exploitation which the latter tolerate serves the capitalists of the former country as a good pretext, sometimes as a real reason, for opposing the efforts of their workers to improve their working conditions through legislation or “free” agreement.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the one way as in the other, it is also made clear to the workers who remain in the country how dependent the progress of their class struggle is on the progress of the working class in other countries. This may temporarily fill them with resentment against the foreign workers, but in the end the insight nonetheless breaks through among them that there is only <em>one</em> effective means of removing the hampering influences of the backwardness of foreign countries: <em>the removal of this backwardness itself.</em> The German workers have every reason to wish, and, as far as it is possible for them, to work towards, that the Slavic and Italian workers, abroad as at home, should attain higher wages and shorter working hours; the English workers have the same interest with regard to the German and others, the American workers with regard to the European workers in general.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The close dependence in which the class struggle of the proletariat of one country stands upon the class struggles of the other countries leads with natural necessity to the close union of the fighting proletarian strata of the various countries.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>More and more the remnants of national seclusion and national hatred, which the proletariat took over from the bourgeoisie, disappear; more and more it frees itself from the national prejudices; more and more the worker learns to recognise and value in the foreign fellow-worker, whatever language he may speak, the comrade in struggle, the comrade.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Most intimate of all, naturally, must the international solidarity become between those proletarian strata of the various nations that have set themselves the same goals and pursue them with the same means.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>How indispensable the international combination of the class struggles of the proletariat is, if they are to go beyond a definite framework in respect of goal, strength and scope, the authors of the <strong>Communist Manifesto</strong> recognised from the outset. It addresses itself to the <em>proletarians of all countries</em> and closes with the appeal to them: “Unite!” And the organisation which they had won over to the principles of the Manifesto, and in whose name it was issued, was an <em>international</em> one, the <em>“League of Communists”.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The consequences of the suppression of the revolutionary movements of 1848 and 1849 put an end to this League, but with the reawakening of the labour movement in the beginnings of the sixties it too arose again in the <em>“International Working Men's Association”</em> (founded 1864), whose soul was once more Marx. Its task was not only to awaken the feeling of international solidarity in the proletarians of the various countries, but also to give them a common goal and to have them take a common path towards it. The former task it fulfilled in ample measure; in the other it partly failed. The “International” was to bring about the union of the fighting proletariat with modern socialism in all countries. It declared that the emancipation of the working classes can be won only by these themselves; that the political movement is a mere means to this goal; and that the emancipation of the proletariat is impossible so long as its dependence on the monopolists of the means of production, the sources of life, continues. Against these principles there arose in the “International” an opposition all the stronger the clearer it became that their consequence was social democracy. All those adherents of bourgeois, petty-bourgeois and primitive-proletarian utopianism, still relatively so numerous 20 and 30 years ago, as well as the representatives of the guild-minded pure-and-simple trade unionism of a labour aristocracy, broke away from the “International” when they noticed where it was heading. The fall of the Commune of Paris in 1871 and the persecutions by the state power in the various countries of the European continent furthered its downfall.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the consciousness of international solidarity, which it had scattered abroad, could not be stifled.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Since then the ideas of the <strong>Communist Manifesto</strong> have seized the fighting proletariat of all Europe (and various proletarian strata outside Europe); everywhere the fusion of the class struggle with modern socialism is either already accomplished or in rapid course of accomplishment. The foundations, goals and means of the proletarian class struggle everywhere become more and more the same. From this there arose of itself an ever closer contact between the socialist labour movements of the various countries; the international consciousness became ever more powerful within them, and it needed only an external impulse to give this fact visible expression.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This, as is well known, happened on the occasion of the hundredth anniversary of the storming of the Bastille, at the international congress of <em>Paris</em> (1889). The congress at <em>Brussels</em> (1891) gave the opportunity to confirm anew the international connection of the fighting proletariat, which moreover receives its visible expression every year in the <em>May Day celebration.</em> It is not isolated thinkers and enthusiasts, regarded as eccentrics by the mass of their class comrades, who come together at these congresses, as at the peace congresses of the bourgeoisie, but the representatives and spokesmen of hundreds of thousands, indeed millions, of working men and women. Every May Day celebration shows in the most impressive manner that it is the <em>masses</em> of the working population who, in all the centres of economic and political life in all the countries of modern culture, are conscious of the international solidarity of the proletariat, who protest against war and declare that the so-called national antagonisms are in fact no longer antagonisms of the peoples, but only antagonisms of their <em>exploiters.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Such a bridging of the gulf between the individual nations, such an international unification of the broadest strata of the people, world history has not yet seen. This process appears all the more magnificent since it takes place in the shadow of a war cloud, which for its part likewise promises to offer a spectacle such as world history has never yet witnessed, a war cloud that threatens Europe with unspeakably horrible devastation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In view of this situation, Social Democracy has the twofold obligation to emphasise its international standpoint decisively. This has also been done in the most emphatic manner in the Erfurt Programme.<br/> <a id="ch5-t14" name="ch5-t14"> </a></p>
```

## 14. Social Democracy and the People

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Social Democracy is, from the very outset and by its entire nature, an international party. But at the same time it also has the tendency to become more and more a <em>national party,</em> that is, a people's party, in the sense that it becomes the representative not merely of the industrial wage labourers, but of all the working and exploited strata, and thus of the great majority of the entire population, of that which one commonly calls the "people." We have already seen that the industrial proletariat has the tendency to become ultimately the sole working class. We have also already pointed out that the other working classes are becoming ever more similar to the proletariat in their standard of living and their working conditions; finally, we already know that the working proletariat is the only one among the working classes which is continually increasing in strength, intelligence, and consciousness of its aim, that it is becoming more and more the centre around which the steadily dwindling remnants of the other working classes group themselves. Its feeling and thinking become ever more decisive for the entire mass of the "little people."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To the extent that the wage labourers take over the leadership of the people, the workers' party becomes a people's party. Indeed, as soon as the independent worker of the small-scale enterprise feels like a proletarian, as soon as he recognises that he, or at the very least his children, are irretrievably fallen prey to the proletariat, that there is no salvation for him other than the emancipation of the proletariat – from that moment on he must see in Social Democracy the natural representative of his interests.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We have already explained in the previous chapter that he has no cause to fear its victory, that this victory rather also lies in his interest, for it means the bringing about of a condition of society which grants to all workers – not merely to the wage labourers, but also to the independent workers of the small-scale enterprises – liberation from exploitation and oppression, the attainment of security and prosperity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But Social Democracy represents the interests of all the "little people" not merely in the <em>future,</em> but already in <em>present-day society.</em> The proletariat, as the lowest of the exploited strata, cannot free itself from exploitation and oppression without destroying every kind of exploitation and oppression. It is therefore the sworn enemy of these, in whatever form they may appear; it is the champion of all the exploited and oppressed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We spoke above of the "International." It is now significant that the occasion for its founding was a proletarian demonstration in favour of the <em>Poles</em> rising up against the yoke of the Tsar; that the first address which the "International" issued after its constitution was an address of congratulation to Lincoln, the President of the United States, in which the workers' society gave expression to its sympathies for the cause of the <em>emancipation of the slaves;</em> and that finally the "International" was the first organisation existing in England and counting Englishmen among its members which took the side of the <em>Irish,</em> mistreated by the ruling classes of England, and did so in the most vigorous manner. Neither the Irish nor the Polish movement, nor the emancipation of the slaves, had directly anything to do with the class interests of the wage labourers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Similar examples could be drawn by the dozen from the history of every socialist workers' movement.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is said, to be sure, that Social Democracy builds upon the progress of economic development; that socialist production has as its precondition the fullest possible displacement of the small-scale enterprise by the large-scale enterprise on all sides. Social Democracy, therefore, has an interest in the downfall of the small-scale enterprises, in the downfall of the small artisans, small traders, small peasants; it must promote their ruin, and can thus impossibly be active in their interest.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To this the following is to be replied: Social Democracy does not <em>make</em> the economic development; the displacement of the small-scale enterprise by the large-scale enterprise is accomplished most thoroughly by the capitalist class without any assistance on its part. It is true that it has no cause to set itself against this development. But to wish to halt economic development in no way means to represent the real interests of the small peasants and petty bourgeois. For all attempts in that direction must fail; insofar as they come to any effect at all, they can only do harm, not benefit. To hold out to the artisans and peasants measures by which their small-scale enterprises are to be made viable in no way means to represent their interests; it rather means to awaken illusions in them which can never be realised and which divert them from the right path to the best representation of their interests.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But even if the downfall of the small-scale enterprise is unavoidable, it is nevertheless by no means unavoidable that this downfall should proceed precisely amid all the hideous accompanying phenomena which as a rule attach to it today. We have seen that the disappearance of a small-scale enterprise is only the final act of a long drama, in whose earlier acts nothing else is played out than the slow and agonising decline of the independent small producer. But Social Democracy not only has not the slightest interest in the small bourgeois and small peasants declining; it rather has the greatest interest that this should not be the case. For the more decayed the circles are from which the proletariat is recruited, the more difficult it is to raise these recruits so far that they become capable and willing to enter the ranks of the <em>fighting proletariat.</em> But upon <em>its</em> expansion, not upon the expansion of the entire proletariat, depend the expansion and strength of Social Democracy. The fewer his needs, the more accustomed to endless labour the peasant and artisan is, the more devoid of resistance he proves to be as soon as he has sunk into the proletariat, the more he lets himself be exploited, the more he harms the higher-standing workers through his competition. In part the same reasons which bring about the international solidarity of the workers also lead to a solidarity of the proletariat with the classes from which it is recruited – a solidarity, to be sure, which up to now has as a rule been felt and practised only from one side, that of the proletarians.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Of course, when the sinking small peasants and petty bourgeois seek to keep themselves above water at the expense of the proletarians, for instance through excessive exploitation of apprentices or through obstructing the organisation of their wage labourers, they will always meet with the energetic resistance of the proletariat and of Social Democracy. On the other hand, the latter enters most decidedly in favour of a series of measures by which, without detriment to the proletariat, but rather with its simultaneous promotion, considerable <em>improvements</em> and <em>reliefs</em> for the small peasant and petty bourgeois are brought about.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This becomes clearly and distinctly evident from the demands which Social Democracy makes upon the present-day state as <em>immediately to be realised.</em> The enumeration of these demands forms the second part of the Erfurt Programme. It reads as follows:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">Proceeding from these principles, the Social Democratic Party of Germany demands first of all:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">1. Universal, equal, direct suffrage and voting rights with secret ballot for all citizens of the Reich over 20 years of age without distinction of sex, for all elections and votes. Proportional representation, and until its introduction, statutory redistricting of the constituencies after every census. Two-year legislative periods. Holding of elections and votes on a statutory day of rest. Compensation for the elected representatives. Abolition of every restriction of political rights except in the case of legal incapacitation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">2. Direct legislation by the people by means of the rights of proposal and rejection. Self-determination and self-government of the people in the Reich, state, province, and municipality. Election of officials by the people, together with their responsibility and liability. Annual granting of taxes.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">3. Education for universal capacity to bear arms. A people's militia in place of the standing armies. Decision over war and peace by the popular representative body. Settlement of all international disputes by means of arbitration.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">4. Abolition of all laws which restrict or suppress the free expression of opinion and the right of association and assembly.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">5. Abolition of all laws which place women at a disadvantage relative to men in matters of public and private law.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">6. Declaration of religion to be a private matter. Abolition of all expenditure from public funds for ecclesiastical and religious purposes. The ecclesiastical and religious communities are to be regarded as private associations which order their affairs entirely independently.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">7. Secular character of the school. Compulsory attendance at the public elementary schools. Free provision of instruction, of teaching materials, and of board in the public elementary schools as well as in the higher educational institutions for those male and female pupils who, by virtue of their abilities, are deemed suited to further education.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">8. Free administration of justice and legal counsel. Adjudication by judges elected by the people. Right of appeal in criminal cases. Compensation for those innocently accused, arrested, and convicted. Abolition of the death penalty.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">9. Free medical assistance, including obstetric care and remedies. Free burial of the dead.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">10. A progressively graduated income and property tax to meet all public expenditure, insofar as this is to be covered by taxes. Obligation of self-assessment. An inheritance tax, graduated progressively according to the size of the estate and according to the degree of kinship. Abolition of all indirect taxes, tariffs, and other economic-policy measures which sacrifice the interests of the general public to the interests of a privileged minority.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">For the protection of the working class, the Social Democratic Party of Germany demands first of all:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">1. An effective national and international labour protection legislation on the following basis:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">a) The fixing of a normal working day amounting to at most eight hours.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">b) Prohibition of gainful employment for children under fourteen years of age.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">c) Prohibition of night work, except for those branches of industry which by their nature, for technical reasons or for reasons of public welfare, require night work.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">d) An uninterrupted rest period of at least 36 hours in every week for every worker.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">e) Prohibition of the truck system.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">2. Supervision of all industrial enterprises, investigation and regulation of working conditions in town and country by an Imperial Labour Office, district labour offices and labour chambers. Thoroughgoing industrial hygiene.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">3. Legal equalisation of agricultural labourers and domestic servants with industrial workers; abolition of the servants' regulations.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">4. Safeguarding of the right of association.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">5. Assumption of the entire workers' insurance by the Reich, with the workers having a decisive share in its administration.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A closer examination of these points does not lie within the plan of this work, which concerns itself merely with the <em>principles</em> of Social Democracy, not with the <em>practical conclusions</em> that follow from them. These conclusions concern us here only in so far as they can serve to illustrate those principles. From this standpoint we shall attach a few brief remarks to them, which are to conclude our work; for we have arrived at the end of the train of thought pursued by the fundamental part of the Erfurt Programme.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Above all it becomes apparent to us that only a part of the demands of Social Democracy are pure workers' demands, that is to say, such demands as are raised exclusively in the interest of the wage proletariat. By far the greatest part of the demands, on the other hand, concerns the sphere of interests which the proletariat has in common with the other strata of the working population.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Some of these demands are also raised by <em>bourgeois</em> democracy; the others, however, can only be raised by Social Democracy as the <em>only</em> anti-capitalist party, and even the bourgeois-democratic demands are championed by no party with the emphasis that it gives them. It seeks to raise the standard of living of the common people and to relieve them by the abolition of indirect taxes, by shifting the greatest burden of taxation onto the rich by means of a progressive income tax, by the abolition of the standing armies, by the elimination of the system of state debts most intimately bound up with them, and so forth. It seeks to raise the common people <em>intellectually</em>, while at the same time relieving them economically, in that it demands not merely a free elementary-school education satisfying all the requirements of modern culture, but also the free provision of teaching materials and the maintenance of the schoolchildren by the community. It alone is the party which wishes to offer the "little man" free, adequate medical assistance and nursing care and free administration of justice, free legal aid.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only measures lying in the direction of these demands are capable of improving the situation of the independent workers of the small-scale enterprises in present-day society as far as is at all possible. To help the artisans and peasants as <em>producers</em> while retaining their backward modes of operation contradicts the course of economic development and is impracticable. It is equally impossible to raise them all, or even a considerable part of them, to the status of capitalists. The <em>mass</em> of these "little people" can be given some help only as <em>consumers</em>. But it is precisely the parties apparently most kindly disposed towards the artisans and peasants that burden them most as consumers. This burden is a real one and painfully perceptible. The raising up of the small-scale enterprises which is supposed to go hand in hand with it, on the other hand, becomes ever more nothing but mere shadow-boxing.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The raising up of the petty bourgeois and small peasants as consumers not only does not contradict economic development, it is a means of promoting it. It is therefore not only possible, but for this very reason to be striven for, quite apart from numerous other reasons which spring partly from rational considerations, partly from the stirrings of compassion with the poor, decaying peasants and petty bourgeois. The better their situation as consumers, the higher their standard of living, the greater their bodily and intellectual claims, the greater their insight, the sooner will they cease to want to wage the struggle against the large-scale enterprise by means of <em>starvation competition</em>, the sooner will they give up the hopeless struggle and strengthen the ranks of the proletariat; but not the ranks of its humble, defenceless, undemanding lowest strata. They will enter directly into the ranks of the fighting, "covetous", purposeful proletarians and thereby accelerate their victory.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This victory will not arise out of degradation, as many have believed; just as little out of the degradation of the petty bourgeoisie and the peasantry as out of that of the proletariat. Social Democracy has every reason to counteract degradation on the one side just as decidedly as on the other, and it does so, too, as far as its powers reach. To strengthen these powers therefore lies not merely in the interest of the wage labourers alone, but equally in the interest of all other members of the population who live from their labour, not from exploitation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The petty bourgeoisie and the peasantry have never really been able, as long as the modern state has existed, to assert their interests by themselves alone against the other classes.
```
