Today they are less able to do so than ever. To safeguard their interests they must combine with one or several other classes. Their instincts, reared by private property, drive them into the arms of the bourgeois parties, that is to say, to combination with one of the various groups of the upper propertied classes. The bourgeois parties themselves seek this combination, partly out of purely party-political needs, because they see in the common people the "voting cattle" that they need, but partly also out of more deeply lying considerations. For they know very well that the private property of the small peasants and petty bourgeois still forms today the strongest support of private property in general, and thereby also of the exploitation which they practise. The <em>prosperity</em> of the little man is a matter of indifference to them. To burden him as a <em>consumer</em>, they are ready at once. Let him decay, so long as only his small-scale enterprise does not vanish entirely, the enterprise which keeps him confined within the circle of private property. Meanwhile all these parties are interested in the extension of capitalist exploitation, that is to say, in the advance of economic development. They may indeed <em>wish</em> to preserve the peasant and the artisan, they may also <em>promise</em> it to him, but <em>in fact</em> they do everything in their power to extend the dominance of the large-scale enterprise and to crush peasant and artisan production.</p>

```{=html}
<p>Quite different is the relationship between the independent workers of the small-scale enterprises and Social Democracy. It cannot, to be sure, advocate the preservation of the small-scale enterprise, but in fact the latter has nothing to fear from it. It is the capitalists and the large landowners, not the proletarians, who expropriate the artisans and peasants. The victory of the proletariat is rather, as we have seen in the previous chapter, the only means of putting an end to these expropriations. As <em>consumers</em>, however, the independent workers of the small-scale enterprises have the same interests as the proletarians. They have therefore every reason to bring about the safeguarding of their interests through combination with the latter, that is to say, through joining Social Democracy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is admittedly not to be expected that this insight will spread among them all too rapidly. Yet the desertion of the petty bourgeois and peasants from the ranks of the bourgeois parties has already begun — a desertion of a quite peculiar kind, for it is precisely the ablest, the most combative, who are the first to throw down their weapons, not in order to escape the turmoil of battle, but in order to flee from the petty struggle to sustain a wretched existence into the gigantic, world-moving struggle for the bringing about of a new society, one that lets all its members share in the great achievements of modern culture; into the struggle for the liberation of the whole of civilised humanity, indeed of the whole of humanity, from the spell of a society that threatens to crush it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more unbearable the existing mode of production becomes, the more evidently it hastens towards its bankruptcy, and the more incapable the ruling parties prove of removing the alarmingly growing social ills; the more unprincipled and lacking in principle these parties become, shrinking ever more into cliques of personally interested politicians — the more numerously will the members of the non-proletarian classes stream to the Social Democracy and, hand in hand with the irresistibly advancing proletariat, follow its banner to victory and triumph.</p>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch5-f9" id="ch5-n9" name="ch5-n9">9.</a> How close the servant and the courtier are to one another is shown to us in amusing fashion by Le Sage’s novel <strong>Gil Blas,</strong> that classic natural history of servitude. On the other side, the servant extends his hand to the lumpenproletarian. Gil Blas rises, without changing in his essential nature, from the lowest servant and companion of rogues to the secretary and favourite of the first minister at the Spanish court.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch5-f10" id="ch5-n10" name="ch5-n10">10.</a> This highly important book <strong>[The Condition of the Working Class in England</strong> – <em>Ed.</em>] was first published in 1845. The third edition of it is now appearing from the publishing house of J.H.W. Dietz in Stuttgart.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch5-f11" id="ch5-n11" name="ch5-n11">11.</a> One may perhaps counter this assertion with the English “labour members,” who have so often betrayed the English working class in Parliament; but these gentlemen owe their election not to the influence of the working class as a whole, but to that of a guild-minded, arrogant labour aristocracy that wishes to raise itself above the proletariat. Messrs. Broadhurst and company have always acted only in the sense of these their principals.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: IV. The State of the Future](ch4.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd)
